turns.content: This JOIN query is taking ~8 seconds on about 2M rows. I’m on PostgreSQL 14. Here’s the query and EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). Can you help optimize with indexes? Query: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, sum(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' GROUP BY o.id, o.created_at, c.email ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) output (abridged): Limit  (cost=... rows=100) (actual time=8200..8210 rows=100 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=12000 read=90000 ->  Sort  (cost=... rows=50000) (actual time=8200..8205 rows=100 loops=1) Sort Key: o.created_at DESC Sort Method: top-N heapsort  Memory: 80kB ->  HashAggregate  (cost=... rows=50000) (actual time=7600..8000 rows=52000 loops=1) Group Key: o.id, o.created_at, c.email Buffers: shared hit=12000 read=90000 ->  Hash Join  (cost=... rows=1000000) (actual time=1200..6200 rows=1500000 loops=1) Hash Cond: (oi.order_id = o.id) Buffers: shared hit=12000 read=90000 ->  Seq Scan on order_items oi  (cost=... rows=8000000) (actual time=0.050..2600 rows=8000000 loops=1) Buffers: shared read=80000 ->  Hash  (cost=... rows=200000) (actual time=1150..1150 rows=180000 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=11000 read=10000 ->  Hash Join  (cost=... rows=200000) (actual time=200..900 rows=180000 loops=1) Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.id) Buffers: shared hit=11000 read=10000 ->  Seq Scan on orders o  (cost=... rows=6000000) (actual time=0.040..400 rows=500000 loops=1) Filter: ((created_at >= (now() - '30 days'::interval)) AND (status = ANY ('{paid,shipped}'::text[]))) Rows Removed by Filter: 5500000 Buffers: shared hit=9000 read=1000 ->  Hash  (cost=... rows=100000) (actual time=190..190 rows=90000 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=2000 read=9000 ->  Seq Scan on customers c  (cost=... rows=1000000) (actual time=0.030..140 rows=90000 loops=1) Filter: (country = 'US'::text) Rows Removed by Filter: 910000 Buffers: shared hit=2000 read=9000 Tables: orders: ~6M rows order_items: ~8M rows customers: ~1M rows Existing indexes: orders_pkey(id) customers_pkey(id) order_items_pkey(id) orders_customer_id_idx (customer_id) order_items_order_id_idx (order_id) What should I add or change?

The plan shows three big costs: 1) Sequential scan on orders filtering out ~5.5M rows to keep ~500k (last 30 days + status). 2) Sequential scan on customers filtering out ~910k to keep ~90k (US). 3) Sequential scan on order_items reading all 8M rows, then hashing and joining to orders. Even though you already have order_items(order_id), the planner chose a seq scan + hash join because it expects joining from a large set of orders to a large set of order_items will be cheaper than many random index lookups. The bottleneck is that you’re not reducing the candidate orders/customers early enough, so it ends up scanning huge portions of all three tables. A few high-impact changes: A) Add a multicolumn index on orders to support the WHERE + ORDER BY + LIMIT pattern. Because you’re filtering by created_at and status and ordering by created_at desc with LIMIT 100, you want an index that can quickly fetch the newest qualifying orders. Typical choice: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_at_id_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id); Rationale: - status is an equality/IN filter; put it first. - created_at supports range and ordering. - id helps make it more selective and can support joins/grouping. - INCLUDE(customer_id) can avoid heap fetches for customer_id in some plans. Alternative if status has low selectivity and created_at is the main driver: CREATE INDEX orders_created_at_desc_status_id_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, status, id) INCLUDE (customer_id); You’ll need to test which yields better plans; the right order depends on data distribution. B) Add an index on customers(country, id) (or a partial index). Since you filter on country and then join on id, an index can help avoid scanning all customers. CREATE INDEX customers_country_id_idx ON customers (country, id); If US is very common (e.g., 70% of customers), this may not help much; but your EXPLAIN suggests only 9% are US, so it’s selective and should help. C) Consider a partial index on orders for the statuses you care about. If most orders are not paid/shipped, a partial index can be much smaller and more effective: CREATE INDEX orders_recent_paid_shipped_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id) WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'); Then the query can use this index for the status filter and for ORDER BY/LIMIT. D) Consider rewriting to “start from the top 100 orders” then join/aggregate, to avoid aggregating 52k groups before LIMIT. Right now, you aggregate many orders and only then apply ORDER BY/LIMIT. But you only need totals for 100 newest orders. A rewrite can drastically reduce work: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This allows the engine to: - use an index to find the 100 newest qualifying orders, - then fetch order_items for just those 100 order_ids (index on order_items(order_id) becomes very effective). E) For order_items, you already have order_items(order_id). If totals are computed often, consider a covering index to reduce heap reads: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_cover_idx ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); This can let the aggregation read from index-only scans if visibility map permits, reducing IO. Questions to confirm before suggesting the exact best index order: 1) What’s the distribution of orders.status? Rough % paid/shipped vs others. 2) How many orders are in the last 30 days (your plan showed ~500k—confirm)? 3) Is created_at correlated with physical order (i.e., mostly append-only), and is the table vacuumed regularly? 4) Is customers.country highly selective (your plan suggests yes)? 5) Do you frequently query totals for many orders, or mostly “latest N orders”?

Status distribution: paid ~25%, shipped ~10%, pending ~40%, cancelled ~25%. Last 30 days is about 450k orders. Orders are mostly insert-only; created_at increases with id. Autovacuum runs, but we do have some bloat. customers.country: US is ~8-10%. And yes, this query is for “latest 100 orders” on a dashboard and runs constantly.

Given “latest 100” dashboard, the biggest win is to change the execution shape so Postgres can stop early. The rewrite that limits orders first is likely the highest ROI, paired with an index that can serve that “latest qualifying orders” access path. Recommended approach: 1) Rewrite query to limit orders first (CTE or subquery), then aggregate items for those orders. 2) Create a partial index on orders for the relevant statuses, ordered by created_at desc. 3) Create a selective index on customers for country filtering. 4) Optionally add covering index on order_items to reduce heap reads. Concrete steps. A) Query rewrite (prefer subquery or CTE that Postgres can inline) In PG14, CTEs are inlineable by default unless MATERIALIZED is used, so you can use CTE for clarity: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country = 'US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This changes the workload from “aggregate ~52k orders then top-N” to “identify 100 orders then aggregate their items”. B) Indexes 1) Partial index on orders for paid/shipped, to support ORDER BY created_at DESC + range on last 30 days: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_paid_shipped_created_desc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id) WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'); Why this shape: - Partial condition matches your status filter and shrinks index size (~35% of rows). - Leading created_at DESC supports ordering; id as tie-breaker. - INCLUDE(customer_id) avoids extra heap fetch for customer_id when building recent_orders. If you sometimes query other statuses too, keep your existing orders_customer_id_idx; it won’t conflict. 2) customers index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY customers_country_id_idx ON customers (country, id) INCLUDE (email); Including email can reduce heap visits when fetching the email for the 100 customers. It’s small but basically free once you’re creating the index. If email is large (text) it increases index size; still probably ok at 1M rows. You can skip INCLUDE(email) if concerned. 3) order_items covering index: You already have order_items(order_id). For the dashboard, you’ll be doing 100 index probes per run, and then reading all matching items. If each order has multiple items, the heap reads can still be significant. A covering index helps: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); This allows index-only scans for aggregation if tuples are all-visible (vacuum). Even without full index-only, the access pattern is more sequential within the index. C) ANALYZE and planner After creating indexes: ANALYZE orders; ANALYZE customers; ANALYZE order_items; Also consider raising default_statistics_target for status/country if estimates are off, but first see if plan improves. D) Expected plan shape For recent_orders CTE: - Index scan on orders_paid_shipped_created_desc_idx with condition created_at >= now()-30d (or it may scan from newest backwards and stop once it found 100 matching after joining to US customers). - Join to customers via primary key, but country filter might be applied using customers_country_id_idx or via filtering after PK lookup. Since you join on c.id, the (country,id) index isn’t directly used for that join direction; however, planner may choose to start from customers where country='US' and join to orders. Which is better depends on selectivity and ability to satisfy ORDER/LIMIT. Often best is start from orders (newest) and check customer country by PK lookup (100 lookups) — cheap. So in practice, the customers(country,id) index may not be critical for the rewritten query, but it can still help in other queries and in the original plan. E) An even better rewrite: semi-join on US customers using EXISTS If you want to force “orders-first” and make country check cheap: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM customers c WHERE c.id = o.customer_id AND c.country = 'US' ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This makes it obvious that customers is only a filter for the 100 candidate orders. If you can share the new EXPLAIN after rewrite + orders partial index, we can fine-tune (e.g., whether it’s using index-only scans, whether it’s still scanning too many orders to find 100 US customers).

I applied the rewrite (EXISTS version) and created the partial index on orders (created_at desc, id) include customer_id where status in ('paid','shipped'). Query time dropped to ~900ms but still higher than I want (goal <200ms). New EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Limit (actual time=890..892 rows=100) -> Sort (top-N heapsort) (actual time=889..891 rows=100) Sort Key: ro.created_at DESC -> HashAggregate (actual time=760..860 rows=100) Group Key: ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email -> Nested Loop (actual time=2..740 rows=1200) -> Nested Loop (actual time=1..20 rows=100) -> Index Scan using orders_paid_shipped_created_desc_idx on orders o (actual time=0.05..8 rows=600 loops=1) Index Cond: (created_at >= now() - '30 days'::interval) Filter: (SubPlan 1) Rows Removed by Filter: 500 SubPlan 1 -> Index Scan using customers_pkey on customers c1 (actual time=0.01..0.01 rows=1 loops=1100) Index Cond: (id = o.customer_id) Filter: (country = 'US') -> Index Scan using customers_pkey on customers c (actual time=0.01..0.01 rows=1 loops=100) Index Cond: (id = o.customer_id) -> Index Scan using order_items_order_id_idx on order_items oi (actual time=0.05..7.0 rows=12 loops=100) Index Cond: (order_id = o.id) Buffers: shared hit=4000 read=6000 It’s scanning 600 orders to get 100 US ones, and then looping to order_items. How can I get it down further?

You’re close; the remaining ~900ms is mostly IO (6000 shared reads) plus 100 index probes into order_items with heap visits. The plan is now “right shaped” (limit early), so the next gains come from (1) reducing reads per order_items lookup and (2) reducing extra work around customers lookups and aggregation. Key observations from your plan: - orders index scan returns 600 rows, filters 500 via EXISTS (i.e., only ~1/6 are US). That’s fine; 600 isn’t large. - customers lookups: 1100 loops in the subplan + 100 loops for final email fetch. Those are cheap if cached, but can still add latency if random IO. - order_items index scan: “actual time 0.05..7.0 rows=12 loops=100” means per order it can take up to ~7ms (likely heap fetches / cache misses). 100 * a few ms gets you into hundreds of ms. So focus on order_items access. 1) Make order_items fetch index-only / reduce heap hits Create a covering index (as mentioned) so the aggregation can use index-only scans: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Then VACUUM (or let autovacuum catch up) so visibility map enables index-only scans: VACUUM (ANALYZE) order_items; Without visibility map, it will still hit heap. But even then, clustering in the index might be better. Check the new plan: you want to see “Index Only Scan” on order_items_order_id_inc_idx, and buffers should shift from reads to hits, and total reads drop. 2) Consider clustering order_items by order_id (optional but can help) If order_items are physically scattered, each order_id lookup can touch many heap pages. Two options: - CLUSTER order_items using order_items_order_id_idx; (heavy operation, rewrites table) - Or a less invasive approach: pg_repack extension to reorder without long locks. Clustering improves locality so each order’s items are near each other on disk, reducing random reads. 3) Pre-aggregate order_items totals in a materialized view or summary table If dashboard needs totals constantly and orders/items are immutable after paid/shipped, precompute totals: Create table order_totals(order_id primary key, total numeric, updated_at) Maintain via trigger on order_items insert/update, or periodic batch. Then query becomes: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, ot.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN orders o on o.id=ro.id JOIN customers c on c.id=o.customer_id JOIN order_totals ot on ot.order_id=o.id ORDER BY o.created_at DESC; This can easily be <200ms because it avoids scanning many order_items rows. If you need real-time totals and order_items change frequently, triggers may be acceptable; otherwise a 1-min refresh is often fine. 4) Reduce double customer lookups You currently hit customers twice: once in EXISTS filter, once to fetch email. You can fold email retrieval into the CTE to avoid second lookup: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country='US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, ro.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, ro.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This might cause the planner to choose a less ideal join order if not careful, but usually it’s fine because LIMIT 100 plus the orders index keeps it small. If it regresses, stick with EXISTS and add a LATERAL to fetch email once. Alternative with LATERAL: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE ... ORDER BY ... LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT email FROM customers c WHERE c.id=ro.customer_id AND c.country='US' ) c ON true JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id=ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; But this still does 100 customer lookups; not the main issue. 5) Check work_mem and hash aggregate HashAggregate is only grouping 100 groups, so it’s not spilling. Not a concern. 6) IO and caching You have shared read=6000. If this is on SSD and cache is cold, 6000 reads can be a lot. With index-only scans, you can cut reads dramatically. Next steps I’d do in order: - Create covering index on order_items (order_id INCLUDE qty,unit_price). - VACUUM (ANALYZE) order_items. - Re-run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). If still heap-heavy, consider clustering/repacking order_items. - If you still can’t hit <200ms consistently, implement order_totals summary table/materialized view. Can you share: average items per order, and whether order_items rows are mostly inserted around order creation time (so naturally clustered) or frequently updated later?

Average 10-15 items per order. order_items inserted at order creation, rarely updated. I created the covering index and vacuumed. New plan shows Index Only Scan on order_items_order_id_inc_idx. Timing now ~320ms. Buffers: hit=9000 read=500. Better but still above 200ms. What else can I do without building a summary table?

If you’re at ~320ms with only ~500 reads, you’re mostly CPU and cache-hit overhead now (plus some residual IO). To shave another ~120ms, you need to reduce per-order overhead further and/or reduce number of order_items rows processed. Options that don’t require a summary table: 1) Avoid per-order index scans by batching order_items access Right now it’s 100 separate index-only scans on order_items. Even if each is fast, 100 * overhead adds up. You can rewrite to fetch items for all 100 orders in a single scan using WHERE order_id IN (...) via a join, which Postgres can execute as a Hash Join or Bitmap Index Scan that batches IO. Example: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM customers c WHERE c.id=o.customer_id AND c.country='US') ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; You already have this, but the planner chose Nested Loop (100 probes). We can encourage a batched plan by disabling nestloop for this query to test, or by rewriting to pre-aggregate order_items for those 100 ids using a semi-join that encourages hashing: WITH recent_orders AS ( ... ), item_totals AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, it.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id=ro.customer_id JOIN item_totals it ON it.order_id=ro.id ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This gives the optimizer freedom to build a hash table of 100 order_ids and scan order_items using a Bitmap/Index scan or even a seq scan (but it should pick bitmap index scan on order_items(order_id) since only 100 ids). With the covering index, bitmap heap isn’t needed; it might do Bitmap Index Scan on the index and then recheck via index-only? Postgres doesn’t do bitmap index-only scan; bitmap leads to heap. So we want either: - Hash join with index scan on order_items (still per-id), or - Merge join if both inputs are ordered by order_id, or - A single Index Scan on order_items using a condition like order_id = ANY(array_of_100_ids) which can be executed as Bitmap Index Scan. Bitmap would force heap visits, which might be worse. So this is tricky: nested loop + index-only might actually be optimal. But you can try a merge join approach: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE ... ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ), sorted_orders AS ( SELECT * FROM recent_orders ORDER BY id ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM sorted_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id=ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; If order_items index is on (order_id) and supports ordered retrieval, planner may do a merge join by scanning the index once across the 100 ids in order. Not guaranteed, but worth checking. 2) Reduce CPU in the aggregate expression SUM(qty * unit_price) requires per-row multiplication. If unit_price is numeric, numeric arithmetic is expensive. If it’s numeric(10,2) or similar, CPU cost can be meaningful at ~100 orders * 15 items = 1500 rows—shouldn’t be huge, but numeric can still add overhead. Check types: - qty is integer? - unit_price is numeric? money? integer cents? If unit_price is numeric, consider storing integer cents and summing qty * unit_price_cents as bigint, then divide in presentation. That can reduce CPU significantly. 3) Avoid sorting at the end You’re sorting 100 rows (top-N heapsort). That should be negligible, but you can make it free by preserving order: If recent_orders produces rows in created_at desc order, and you join/aggregate without reordering, Postgres may still need to sort after grouping. You can use GROUP BY on ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email; aggregation can disturb order. However, you can do aggregation in a LATERAL subquery per order to avoid global group/sort: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE ... ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, it.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id=ro.customer_id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = ro.id ) it ON true ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This removes GROUP BY and HashAggregate entirely and replaces it with 100 small aggregates (still 100 index-only scans). But it might be faster because it avoids hashing/grouping overhead and can keep the order without final sort. Often this is a win for “top N with per-row aggregates”. Given you already do 100 index-only scans, LATERAL might cut overhead enough to go from 320ms to <200ms. 4) Tune random_page_cost / effective_cache_size If planner choices are good already, this won’t change runtime much. But if you see occasional regressions, set appropriate values for SSD: - random_page_cost ~1.1-1.5 - effective_cache_size to reflect RAM available for caching This can help planner consistently pick index-based plans. 5) Connection-level overhead If the 320ms includes network latency or app overhead, confirm you’re measuring server execution time (EXPLAIN ANALYZE) vs end-to-end. Your EXPLAIN shows ~320ms, so it’s server-side. I’d try the LATERAL rewrite first; it’s simple and often effective for dashboards. Can you share column types for qty and unit_price, and whether unit_price is numeric?

qty is int, unit_price is numeric(10,2). I tried the LATERAL rewrite; EXPLAIN ANALYZE now shows ~190ms. That meets goal. But I’m worried about scalability—will 100 lateral subqueries be worse when it’s 500 rows? Also, any indexing changes needed?

LATERAL here is effectively “do a small aggregate per order_id”. With an index-only scan on order_items(order_id) including qty,unit_price, it scales roughly with: - number of selected orders (N) - average items per order (k) Total rows processed ~ N*k. For N=100 and k=15, that’s ~1500 rows—easy. For N=500, ~7500 rows—still not huge. The overhead is the repeated index scan setup (500 times), but each scan is narrow and index-only. On modern hardware, 500 index-only probes is usually fine, especially if pages are cached. If you ever push N into tens of thousands, then you’d want a set-based aggregation rather than per-row subqueries. But for dashboard limits like 100–500, LATERAL is a good pattern. Indexing changes: - Keep order_items_order_id_inc_idx (order_id INCLUDE qty,unit_price). That’s the key index for the LATERAL approach. - Keep orders_paid_shipped_created_desc_idx (created_at desc, id) INCLUDE customer_id WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'). Optionally: add INCLUDE(status) is unnecessary because it’s in the predicate, not selected. created_at and id are selected; customer_id needed to join. Customers: - For the LATERAL query, you’re doing 100 PK lookups on customers. That’s fine. If you also filter by country, do it in the recent_orders CTE to avoid extra lookups or include it in EXISTS. With LATERAL, you can keep the EXISTS filter and then join to customers for email (100 lookups). No extra index needed beyond PK. Scalability considerations / gotchas: 1) Cache behavior: If order_items index pages are not in cache, 500 probes can cause random IO. But your buffers read dropped dramatically already; if the dashboard runs frequently, cache will stay warm. 2) numeric arithmetic: numeric(10,2) is slower than integer math. At 7500 rows it’s still fine, but if you need extra headroom, consider storing unit_price_cents as integer in future schema revisions. 3) Autovacuum: index-only scans depend on visibility map. Ensure autovacuum keeps up on order_items; otherwise it will fall back to heap fetches and latency rises. Monitor pg_stat_user_tables (n_dead_tup) and autovacuum settings. If you want a safer plan for N=500 without relying on many subqueries, you can keep the set-based aggregation version and see if it matches performance, but since you hit 190ms already, I’d keep LATERAL. If you share your final LATERAL query text and its EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS), I can sanity-check that it is indeed using Index Only Scan and not doing extra sorts.

Final query: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM customers c WHERE c.id=o.customer_id AND c.country='US') ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, it.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id=ro.customer_id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = ro.id ) it ON true ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; EXPLAIN shows Index Only Scan on order_items_order_id_inc_idx and total time 185-200ms. Looks good. Any further micro-optimizations? Maybe reduce the customers EXISTS overhead?

A few micro-optimizations you can consider, but you’re already in a good place: 1) Collapse the EXISTS + join to customers into a single lookup (avoid two customer accesses) Right now you check country via EXISTS (100–600 PK lookups during candidate search) and then do another 100 PK lookups to fetch email. You can fetch both country and email in one join inside recent_orders, but you were earlier cautious about join order. With the orders partial index and LIMIT, it’s usually fine. Try: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.country='US' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, ro.email, it.total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = ro.id ) it ON true ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; This reduces customer lookups to exactly 100 (for the chosen orders), but it may require scanning more orders to find 100 US ones anyway; that scanning already required customer checks, so net improvement depends on plan. To preserve “orders-first” while still avoiding duplicate lookup, you can use a LATERAL customer fetch in the CTE: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id, c.email FROM orders o JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT email FROM customers c WHERE c.id=o.customer_id AND c.country='US' ) c ON true WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) ... This keeps the logic “probe customers for each candidate order” but you only probe once per candidate and carry email forward. 2) Ensure the orders index supports fast backward scan and minimal heap fetch Your orders index includes customer_id; you still need id and created_at (in index keys). If the query reads only those plus status predicate satisfied by partial index, it can be index-only on orders too if visibility map is good. But since you need created_at and customer_id, it already has them. If you see heap fetches on orders in EXPLAIN, you can VACUUM orders or accept it (it’s only hundreds of rows). 3) Use timestamptz comparisons carefully created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' is fine. If you want to avoid function volatility concerns, you can pass a parameter from application (cutoff timestamp) so the planner can use a stable value; but with index scan it’s already good. 4) Consider adding id to ORDER BY for deterministic ordering and possible index usage If created_at has ties and you want deterministic results: ORDER BY o.created_at DESC, o.id DESC Then ensure the index is (created_at DESC, id DESC). You already have (created_at DESC, id). This can avoid extra work if there are many same-timestamp rows. Not usually a big deal. 5) Reduce numeric overhead (optional) If you can’t change schema, you can cast to numeric less? Not really. But you can do: SUM(oi.qty::numeric * oi.unit_price) This probably doesn’t help. The main win would be storing integer cents. 6) Prepared statements and generic plans If you parameterize the cutoff, make sure you’re not getting a generic plan that ignores selectivity. For this query, it’s stable, so probably fine. But if you see plan instability, consider: SET plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; (session-level) for testing. Given you’re already at 185–200ms, the simplest improvement is the “single customer lookup” version. I’d test both variants and compare: - runtime - buffers read/hit - number of loops into customers If the join version regresses (starts from customers), keep current. If you paste the EXPLAIN for the customer-join-in-CTE variant, we can see whether it stays orders-driven.

Tried the LATERAL customer fetch in recent_orders; runtime is about the same. I think we’re done. One last question: should I keep both order_items_order_id_idx and order_items_order_id_inc_idx, or drop the old one?

If order_items_order_id_inc_idx is on (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price), it can serve everything the plain (order_id) index does for lookups by order_id, plus it’s larger. Whether to keep both depends on workload: - If you have other queries that only need order_id and benefit from a smaller index (better cache residency, faster maintenance), the plain index can still be useful. - But Postgres can use the covering index anywhere it would use the plain one (same leading key). So functionally it’s redundant. Trade-offs: - Keeping both increases write overhead (inserts into order_items update two indexes) and increases storage. - Dropping the plain one may slightly slow queries that only need order_id because the covering index is bigger, but often the difference is negligible. Given order_items is insert-heavy at order creation and you care about dashboard latency, I’d lean toward dropping the redundant plain index unless you’ve measured it helps: Check index usage: SELECT relname, indexrelname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read, idx_tup_fetch FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE relname='order_items' ORDER BY idx_scan DESC; If order_items_order_id_idx has low/no scans after deploying the new index, drop it: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY order_items_order_id_idx; Keep the primary key index, of course. Also consider maintenance: the INCLUDE index is larger; ensure autovacuum and reindex strategy if bloat occurs.
Great. Thanks for the help.

While we’re on optimization: I have another slow query on the same database. It’s a search endpoint with optional filters. It’s fast sometimes but can jump to 3-5 seconds. How do I handle “optional filter” queries in Postgres without creating a million indexes?

The big issue with “optional filters” is plan instability: Postgres may generate a generic plan (especially with prepared statements) that’s OK on average but terrible for particular parameter combinations, or it may pick a plan based on estimates that don’t match reality (correlated columns, skew). A structured approach that avoids “a million indexes”: 1) Decide which parameter combinations must be fast Usually it’s a few common paths: - “search by date range + status” - “search by customer_id” - “search by email prefix” - “search by order_id” Don’t try to optimize every combination equally. 2) Use query shapes that let the planner choose the best index The classic anti-pattern is: WHERE ($1 IS NULL OR col = $1) AND ($2 IS NULL OR col2 = $2) This can prevent index usage or lead to generic plans. Instead, generate SQL dynamically in the app to include only predicates that are present. That produces a query with simple predicates and stable selectivity. If you can’t do dynamic SQL, consider multiple prepared statements for common paths. 3) Avoid generic plans for highly variable selectivity If you use prepared statements and see “sometimes 5 seconds”, you might be getting a generic plan. For a test session: SET plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; If that fixes the slow cases, you can: - disable prepare for that endpoint in your driver, or - use separate prepared statements per filter set, or - force custom plans at session level (not always desirable globally). 4) Use a small set of targeted composite/partial indexes Instead of indexing every column, index the “leading” constraints that define the search. Partial indexes are great when you have common predicates like status in (...) or “active=true”. 5) Extended statistics for correlated filters If filters are correlated (e.g., status and created_at, country and state, etc.), create extended stats so estimates improve: CREATE STATISTICS orders_status_created_stats (dependencies) ON status, created_at FROM orders; ANALYZE orders; This can prevent the planner from choosing an obviously wrong join type or scan type. If you paste the slow query (with a couple example parameter sets: fast vs slow) and one EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) from a slow case, I can suggest the minimal set of indexes + whether you’re suffering from generic plans.

Query is roughly: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE ($1::timestamptz IS NULL OR o.created_at >= $1) AND ($2::timestamptz IS NULL OR o.created_at < $2) AND ($3::text IS NULL OR o.status = $3) AND ($4::text IS NULL OR c.email ILIKE $4 || '%') ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50; We prepare it in the app. Fast case: only date range set (last 7 days). Slow case: email prefix set to something rare, and date range null. In slow case it does a huge scan.

That pattern is exactly where generic plans hurt: when some parameters are NULL, the OR predicates become “always true” and the planner can’t reliably pick a selective index path for any specific call. Fixes in priority order: A) Stop using the “($param IS NULL OR predicate)” pattern for prepared statements Generate SQL dynamically so the rare email-prefix case becomes: ... WHERE c.email ILIKE $1 || '%' ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50; and the date-range case becomes: ... WHERE o.created_at >= $1 AND o.created_at < $2 ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50; If you can’t do fully dynamic SQL, at least split into a few prepared statements: 1) email-prefix present 2) email-prefix absent and optionally variants for status/date. B) Use force_custom_plan as a diagnostic; likely confirms generic plan issue In one session: SET plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; Then run the prepared statement with the “rare email prefix” parameters. If it becomes fast, you’ve confirmed it. C) Add the right indexes for the main paths 1) For date-range + order by created_at desc: You already have a partial paid/shipped index for the dashboard. For this endpoint (all statuses), consider: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_created_at_desc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id, status); If most calls include a date range, this is a good general index. 2) For email prefix search: ILIKE with a prefix can use an index only with the right operator class. Two good options: Option 1: citext + btree (best if you can change type) - Convert customers.email to CITEXT (case-insensitive). - Then a normal btree index supports prefix LIKE: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY customers_email_citext_idx ON customers (email); And query becomes: WHERE c.email LIKE $1 || '%' Option 2: pg_trgm (works with ILIKE, no type change) Enable extension: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm; Index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY customers_email_trgm_idx ON customers USING gin (email gin_trgm_ops); Then ILIKE 'prefix%' can use it well, and also supports contains searches. But: even if you find matching customers quickly, you then need the newest 50 orders for those customers. That can still be expensive unless you can join efficiently. 3) Join strategy for email prefix case If prefix is rare, best plan is: - find matching customer ids via email index - then find their orders quickly (using orders(customer_id, created_at desc)) You already have orders_customer_id_idx (customer_id). For “newest orders per customer(s)”, a composite helps: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_customer_created_desc_idx ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (status); This index makes “orders for these customers ordered by created_at” cheap. D) Rewrite for the email-prefix case to make the join order obvious When email filter is present, you can structure query as: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.status, c.email FROM ( SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE email ILIKE $1 || '%' ) c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50; This encourages “customers first, then orders” which is correct when the prefix is selective. With orders_customer_created_desc_idx, it’s usually very fast. E) If you must keep a single prepared statement If dynamic SQL isn’t possible, you can sometimes salvage it by using UNION ALL with mutually exclusive branches, so each branch has sargable predicates: SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE $4 IS NULL AND (date/status predicates) UNION ALL SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE $4 IS NOT NULL AND (email predicate) AND (date/status predicates) ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50; Each branch can get its own plan that uses indexes. This is a common workaround for optional predicates with prepared statements. If you tell me whether you can change application SQL generation, I can recommend either (1) split statements / dynamic SQL, or (2) UNION ALL approach plus the smallest index set.

We can generate SQL dynamically. For email, we can also use citext; I’d rather avoid trigram if possible. If I add orders(customer_id, created_at desc) and customers(email) with citext, do I still need customers_country_id_idx from earlier?

If customers_country_id_idx was only created to help the original slow “US customers” filter and you’re now using the rewritten dashboard query (orders-first + EXISTS) that only probes ~600 customers by PK, then customers_country_id_idx is probably not pulling its weight. So: - For the dashboard query: customers_country_id_idx is not necessary; PK lookups + country filter are cheap at that scale. - For the search endpoint: customers(email) (as citext) is useful, and customers_country_id_idx doesn’t help that. Before dropping, verify it’s not used elsewhere: SELECT indexrelname, idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE relname='customers' ORDER BY idx_scan DESC; If customers_country_id_idx has near-zero usage, drop it to reduce write overhead: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY customers_country_id_idx; On the citext approach: - Convert column: ALTER TABLE customers ALTER COLUMN email TYPE citext; (Consider downtime / lock; often it’s quick but test.) - Index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY customers_email_idx ON customers (email); - Query uses: WHERE c.email LIKE $1 || '%' (no ILIKE needed) Then add: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_customer_created_desc_idx ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (status); That combination typically makes the “rare email prefix, no date range” case fast because it becomes: find a small set of customers by email prefix, then pull their newest orders via the composite index, stopping at LIMIT 50.
Makes sense. For the dynamic SQL, do you recommend separate indexes for status filtering too? Sometimes the endpoint filters status only (no date, no email), and that can still scan a lot.

If “status only” is a common call and you need it fast, you have two realistic options: 1) If results are ordered by created_at DESC with LIMIT 50, make an index that matches that access pattern per status. Because the query wants “newest 50 orders with status = X”, the best index is: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_status_created_desc_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id); That lets Postgres jump straight to the newest rows for a given status and stop after 50. This is usually much better than a standalone status index. If you already have your partial paid/shipped created_at index for the dashboard, keep it; it’s smaller and specialized. The general status+created_at index covers all statuses for the search endpoint. 2) Partial indexes for the handful of statuses that are actually queried If only a couple statuses are used in the UI (e.g., ‘pending’ and ‘paid’), partial indexes can be smaller: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_pending_created_desc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id) WHERE status = 'pending'; But with 4 statuses and your distribution, the general composite (status, created_at) is usually simpler and good enough. Given you said “sometimes status only”, I’d add orders_status_created_desc_idx unless index bloat/write overhead is a concern.
OK. One more subtlety: for date range queries, we filter created_at >= start and < end and order by created_at desc. Would an index on (created_at desc) alone be enough, or should I include id too?

Use (created_at DESC, id) rather than just created_at. Reasons: - Tie-breaking: if many rows share the same created_at (common if it’s second-resolution), the planner may need extra work to get a stable top-N and avoid rechecking lots of heap tuples. - It improves index ordering uniqueness, which helps with LIMIT queries and can reduce “fetch extra then filter” behavior. - It can also help with pagination if you ever do “seek method” (created_at, id) < (last_created_at, last_id). So: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_created_desc_id_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id) INCLUDE (customer_id, status); But if you also want status-only fast, you’d typically prefer the combined status+created index, and might not need the created_at-only index. Too many overlapping indexes can hurt writes. A common minimal set for your use cases: - orders_paid_shipped_created_desc_idx (partial) for dashboard - orders_status_created_desc_idx for search endpoint (covers status-only and status+date) - orders_customer_created_desc_idx for email-prefix -> customers -> orders path Then you likely don’t need an additional created_at-only index.
Got it. For customers email search with citext, any gotchas with LIKE and indexes?

A few: - Make sure the pattern is left-anchored: email LIKE 'abc%'. That’s indexable with btree. - Avoid leading wildcard: '%abc' won’t use btree (would need trigram). - Ensure collation is compatible. With non-C collations, index usage for LIKE can be limited. If your database uses a locale collation (common), prefix LIKE can still use btree in many cases, but not always as efficiently. If you run into issues, one robust approach is to use the “C” collation for the index: CREATE INDEX ... ON customers ((email COLLATE "C")); and query with same collation. This is more niche; only do it if you see the planner not using the index. - citext makes comparisons case-insensitive, but LIKE semantics with citext are also case-insensitive, which is what you want. Test with: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) SELECT id FROM customers WHERE email LIKE 'rareprefix%'; You want an Index Scan / Bitmap Index Scan on customers_email_idx.
Perfect. I’ll implement those. If I see bloat from multiple indexes on orders, any maintenance tips?

For bloat management without heavy downtime: - Ensure autovacuum is keeping up (especially on orders and order_items). Monitor: pg_stat_user_tables: n_dead_tup, last_autovacuum - Consider lowering autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor for big, frequently updated tables (orders might get status updates): ALTER TABLE orders SET (autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01); Adjust based on write rate. - Periodic REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on the worst-bloated indexes (PG14 supports it): REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY orders_status_created_desc_idx; - If table bloat is severe, pg_repack is the usual tool to rewrite with minimal locking. - Keep index count minimal and avoid overlapping indexes that don’t buy you measurable wins. If you post pg_stat_user_indexes after a week of production traffic, you can identify unused indexes and drop them safely.
Thanks—this is exactly what I needed.
Can you walk me through why Dijkstra's is O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue?

Dijkstra’s algorithm has two main kinds of work: 1) Priority-queue operations (extract-min and decrease-key / insert) 2) Edge relaxations (scanning adjacency lists) Assume a graph with V vertices and E edges, stored as adjacency lists. High-level flow: - Initialize distance array: O(V) - Push the source into a min-priority queue keyed by distance. - Repeatedly: a) Extract the vertex u with smallest tentative distance (extract-min) b) For each outgoing edge (u, v) with weight w, attempt relaxation: if dist[u] + w < dist[v], update dist[v] and update its priority in the queue. Now the complexity details with a binary heap: - You do extract-min at most V times (once per vertex when it’s finalized). Each extract-min is O(log V) ⇒ total O(V log V). - You consider each edge once (in adjacency list). For each edge, you might do a decrease-key (or push a new entry) when you find a better distance. Each decrease-key/insert is O(log V). In the worst case, a relaxation improves dist[v] for many edges, but it can happen at most O(E) times overall across all edges (each successful relaxation corresponds to one decrease-key/insert). That yields O(E log V). Combine: O(V log V + E log V) = O((V+E) log V). This is the typical bound when using a binary heap and either a true decrease-key or the common “lazy” approach of inserting duplicates and skipping stale entries (still O(E log V) inserts + O(E log V) pops in worst-case-ish bounds, but typically summarized as O((V+E) log V) for the decrease-key model; the lazy model is often stated as O(E log E) which is O(E log V) for simple graphs where E ≤ V^2, but the details can be nuanced).
I get the rough idea, but I’m confused about “extract-min at most V times.” If we do the lazy approach with duplicates, can’t we extract more than V times?

Yes—good distinction. Two variants: A) Decrease-key variant (conceptual textbook) - Each vertex appears once in the heap. - When a better distance is found, you decrease its key in place. - Then you extract-min exactly V times (each vertex once). B) Lazy “insert duplicates” variant (common in practice with binary heaps that don’t support decrease-key nicely) - Every time you find a better distance to v, you push a new (dist, v) entry. - The heap can contain multiple entries for the same vertex. - You still “finalize” each vertex once, but you may pop stale entries too. In the lazy variant, number of extract-min operations can exceed V because you might pop stale entries. How many pops can there be? - Each push corresponds to a potential later pop. - You push at most once per successful relaxation, which is O(E) pushes. - Therefore you can pop O(E) times (worst-case), each pop O(log Nheap). Nheap ≤ O(E). So complexity can be written as O(E log E) for the heap ops. Since log E is Θ(log V^2) = Θ(2 log V) = Θ(log V) in dense graphs, people often simplify to O(E log V). But strictly, with lazy duplicates it’s O((E + V) log E) or O(E log E). The clean O((V+E) log V) statement typically assumes decrease-key or assumes log E ~ log V in big-O terms.
Can you show the exact counting argument for the decrease-key version? Like how many decrease-keys can happen?

Sure. Let’s define the operations and bound each: Setup: - dist array initialization: O(V) - heap build: Option 1: Insert each vertex with initial key (∞), then decrease-key source to 0 Option 2: Insert only source initially Either way, initialization is O(V) or O(V log V) depending on implementation, but it doesn’t dominate the main term. Core loop: While heap not empty: 1) extract-min: removes one vertex u with smallest key among unsettled vertices. 2) relax all outgoing edges of u: For each (u, v) in adjacency list: if dist[u] + w < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + w decrease-key(v, dist[v]) Now count: - extract-min: Each iteration finalizes one new vertex (in classic Dijkstra with nonnegative weights, once extracted, its distance is final). There are V vertices ⇒ at most V extract-min operations. Each extract-min is O(log V) ⇒ O(V log V). - relaxations: For adjacency list, you iterate over each edge exactly once when scanning edges from its source vertex (or twice if undirected stored as two directed edges). Total edge scans = O(E). - decrease-key: You call decrease-key only when relaxation succeeds (strictly decreases dist[v]). How many successful relaxations can happen? Each edge can cause at most one successful relaxation at the moment it’s considered, but across the algorithm, a vertex’s distance can be decreased multiple times via different incoming edges as better paths are found. The key observation: each successful relaxation corresponds to discovering a strictly smaller dist for some vertex, and each such event is charged to an edge examination that succeeded. Since each edge is examined once, there can be at most E successful relaxations total. Therefore total decrease-key calls ≤ E. Each decrease-key is O(log V) ⇒ O(E log V). Total: O(V log V + E log V + E) = O((V+E) log V).

Wait, “each edge is examined once” but an edge might be examined when its source is extracted. That happens once per vertex, okay. But why does that limit successful relaxations to E? A vertex could be improved multiple times by different edges, but that still seems <= number of edges. Is that the whole story?

That’s essentially the whole story for the count: successful relaxations are a subset of edge examinations. More explicitly: - For each directed edge e = (u, v), there is exactly one moment when the algorithm considers e: when u is extracted (settled) and you scan u’s adjacency list. - At that moment, either e causes an improvement of dist[v] (success) or it doesn’t (failure). - Therefore each edge contributes at most 1 successful relaxation. - Summing over all edges: total successful relaxations ≤ E. So even though a vertex v might be decreased many times, each decrease corresponds to a distinct incoming edge that succeeded at its scan time. The total across all vertices is still bounded by the total number of edges scanned.
That makes sense. But then why do some sources say O(E log V) and omit the V log V part?

Because for connected graphs with E ≥ V−1, the E log V term dominates V log V asymptotically: - If E is Ω(V), then (V+E) log V = Θ(E log V). So they just write O(E log V) for simplicity. But in very sparse graphs (like a forest-ish structure), V log V and E log V are the same order anyway because E is still Θ(V) up to a constant factor if connected. For disconnected graphs, you may run Dijkstra from a source and only reach part of the graph; then you can parameterize by the reachable subgraph sizes.
Can we talk about the data structure choice? Like Fibonacci heap gives O(E + V log V). How does that happen?

With Fibonacci heaps (amortized bounds): - insert: O(1) amortized - decrease-key: O(1) amortized - extract-min: O(log V) amortized Dijkstra does: - V extract-min operations ⇒ O(V log V) - ≤ E decrease-key operations ⇒ O(E) So total O(E + V log V). That’s the classic theoretical improvement. In practice, Fibonacci heaps have large constants and complexity overhead; binary heaps or pairing heaps often win.
Pairing heap complexity is fuzzy. What bound can we claim for Dijkstra with a pairing heap?

Pairing heaps have: - insert: O(1) amortized (often stated) - decrease-key: not as clean; there are bounds like O(log V) amortized under certain analyses, but historically it was an open problem for a while; modern results give amortized bounds but they’re more complex than Fibonacci’s. For interviews, a safe statement: - Using a binary heap: O((V+E) log V) - Using Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) amortized - Pairing heap: often performs well, with decrease-key “amortized sublogarithmic” claims in some contexts, but unless you’ve studied the exact results, keep it qualitative.
Let’s anchor on binary heap. Can you walk through a small example and show where the log factor comes from concretely?

Take a tiny graph with V = 5 vertices. A binary heap storing (distance, vertex) is a complete binary tree in array form. Operations maintain heap order by “bubbling” an element up or down along a root-to-leaf path whose length is O(log V). Example: - Suppose heap size is 5. Height is floor(log2 5) ≈ 2. - extract-min removes the root, moves last element to root, then sift-down. Each sift step compares with children and swaps; number of steps bounded by heap height ⇒ O(log V). - decrease-key reduces a key; it may violate heap order with parent; bubble-up swaps along path to root ⇒ O(log V). So each heap op costs proportional to height, which is logarithmic in number of elements. In Dijkstra, each successful relaxation triggers decrease-key, and each vertex finalization triggers extract-min. Hence those log factors multiply counts.
I’ve seen another expression: O(E log V) if you use adjacency list and min-heap, but O(V^2) if you use adjacency matrix and no heap. Can you explain that tradeoff?

Yes. Adjacency matrix approach (classic “simple” Dijkstra): - dist array initialized. - Maintain a visited set. - Repeat V times: 1) Find the unvisited vertex u with minimum dist[u] by scanning all V vertices: O(V). 2) Mark u visited. 3) Relax edges from u: with adjacency matrix, you might scan all possible neighbors v (1..V) to check if edge (u,v) exists and relax: O(V). Total per iteration O(V + V) = O(V), repeated V times ⇒ O(V^2). Adjacency list + heap: - Finding min dist vertex becomes extract-min: O(log V) instead of O(V). - Relaxing neighbors uses adjacency lists, so total neighbor scans is O(E) instead of O(V^2). Total O((V+E) log V). So: - Dense graphs (E ~ V^2): adjacency matrix O(V^2) can be competitive or better than O(E log V) = O(V^2 log V). - Sparse graphs: heap+adjacency list wins.
When would you say Dijkstra is O(V^2) even with adjacency list?

If you implement the “find min unvisited vertex” step by linear scan of dist each time (no heap), then: - V iterations * O(V) min-scan = O(V^2) - plus relaxing adjacency lists O(E) Total O(V^2 + E) = O(V^2) since E ≤ V^2. So adjacency list alone doesn’t guarantee better complexity; the priority queue is the key for the improved bound.
Can you define Big-O precisely? Sometimes I feel people wave hands with dropping constants and lower-order terms.

Big-O is an asymptotic upper bound. Formally, f(n) ∈ O(g(n)) if there exist constants c > 0 and n0 such that for all n ≥ n0: f(n) ≤ c · g(n). Key points: - It’s about behavior for sufficiently large n. - Constants multiply and lower-order additive terms are ignored because they don’t affect the existence of such c and n0. - It’s an upper bound, not necessarily tight. For algorithms, n might represent V, E, or combined parameters. Then you can have multi-parameter big-O like O((V+E) log V).
Multi-parameter big-O trips me up. Is O((V+E) log V) the same as O(V log V + E log V)? And is it okay to say O(E log V) always?

Yes, O((V+E) log V) = O(V log V + E log V) by distributivity. But saying O(E log V) “always” is not strictly equivalent unless you assume E dominates V (like connected graphs with E ≥ V−1). If you allow E to be tiny compared to V (e.g., E = 0), then: - (V+E) log V ≈ V log V - E log V = 0 So you can’t drop V log V without assumptions. In many graph contexts, especially shortest path in a connected graph, E is at least V−1, so it’s common to simplify.
Good. Now, can we compare Dijkstra to BFS and Bellman-Ford in complexity terms?

Sure, for single-source shortest paths: BFS (unweighted graph or all weights equal): - Uses a queue. - Visits each vertex once, scans each edge once. - Time: O(V+E) - Space: O(V) Dijkstra (nonnegative weights): - With binary heap: O((V+E) log V) - With Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) - Space: O(V+E) for adjacency lists + O(V) for dist/parent + PQ overhead Bellman-Ford (can handle negative weights, detect negative cycles reachable from source): - Relaxes all edges V−1 times: O(VE) - Plus one more pass to detect negative cycle: O(E) So total O(VE) So: - BFS fastest but only for unweighted (or unit-weight). - Dijkstra handles nonnegative weights efficiently. - Bellman-Ford handles negatives but slower.
If all edge weights are 0 or 1, there’s also 0-1 BFS, right? Complexity?

Yes. For weights in {0,1}, you can use a deque: - When relaxing an edge of weight 0, push the neighbor to the front. - Weight 1, push to the back. This maintains a monotonic-like property similar to Dijkstra but with O(1) deque ops. Time: O(V+E) Space: O(V) This is often described as Dijkstra specialized to 0/1 weights.
Coming back to Dijkstra, could you show pseudocode that corresponds to the O((V+E) log V) analysis?

One standard version (decrease-key conceptual): Initialize dist[v] = ∞ for all v dist[s] = 0 Initialize min-heap PQ with all vertices keyed by dist[v] while PQ not empty: u = extractMin(PQ) for each edge (u, v, w) in Adj[u]: if dist[u] + w < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + w decreaseKey(PQ, v, dist[v]) Complexity: - Building heap with V elements: O(V) with heapify, or O(V log V) with repeated inserts (often not dominant). - extractMin: V times → O(V log V) - for loop scans edges: total O(E) - decreaseKey: ≤ E times → O(E log V) Total: O((V+E) log V).
If we don’t want to insert all vertices initially, we can insert just the source. Does that change complexity?

It can tweak constants and the exact form, but asymptotically it stays similar. Lazy insert-only-when-discovered approach (still with decrease-key if you keep one entry per vertex): - Start PQ with (0, s). - When you relax to a new vertex v for the first time, insert it. - Later improvements use decrease-key. Then: - Number of inserts ≤ V - Number of extract-min ≤ V (reachable vertices) - Number of decrease-key ≤ E (edges scanned that improve) So still O((V+E) log V) with binary heap, using V as reachable vertices.
How does the “once extracted, finalized” property connect to correctness and complexity? Is it needed for the V extract-min bound?

Yes, it’s what justifies that each vertex is extracted at most once in the decrease-key model. Correctness sketch (nonnegative weights): - Dijkstra maintains a set S of settled vertices whose shortest distance is known. - At each step, it picks u outside S with minimum tentative dist[u]. - Because all edge weights are nonnegative, any alternative path to u through other unsettled vertices would have length ≥ dist[u]. So dist[u] is final when chosen. Therefore u never needs to be reconsidered/updated after being extracted. So exactly one extract-min per vertex. Complexity impact: - Without that property, you might need to reinsert/re-extract vertices many times (as in some algorithms for graphs with negative weights). Dijkstra’s efficiency relies on monotonicity from nonnegative weights.
In the lazy duplicate approach, even though we might pop stale entries, we still only “settle” each vertex once, right?

Right. Typical pattern: - When you pop (d, u) from PQ: - If d != dist[u], it’s stale; skip. - Else, process u’s outgoing edges (and optionally mark visited/settled). With nonnegative weights, the first time you pop a vertex with d == dist[u], that is the final shortest distance, and you can mark it settled and avoid processing it again. Later stale pops for u (with larger d) get skipped. So “settled once” still holds; “popped” may happen multiple times.
I want to be careful: in lazy approach, could there be a case where we pop u with d == dist[u], settle it, and later find an even shorter path to u?

Not with nonnegative edge weights and the standard algorithm that always pops the smallest d available. That’s essentially the correctness argument: - If u is popped with the smallest tentative distance among all unsettled vertices, and weights are nonnegative, no later relaxation can reduce u further. If negative weights exist, then yes, you can settle too early and later discover a shorter path, which is why Dijkstra doesn’t work with negative weights.
Let’s shift to complexity of other graph algorithms. For example, DFS and BFS are O(V+E). Why not O(E) or O(V)?

Because they touch both vertices and edges: - You typically initialize visited array for V vertices: O(V). - You scan adjacency lists: each edge is considered once (directed) or twice (undirected representation): O(E). So total O(V+E). If you know the graph is connected and E ≥ V−1, then O(V+E) = O(E). But without that assumption, you keep both terms.
For topological sort using DFS, same complexity?

Yes: - DFS visits each vertex once: O(V) - Scans each edge once: O(E) So O(V+E). Kahn’s algorithm: - Compute indegrees: scanning edges O(E) - Queue operations per vertex O(V) - Decrement indegrees per edge O(E) Total O(V+E).
Back to interview-style questions: Sometimes they ask “What’s the complexity of Dijkstra?” but don’t specify data structures. How should I answer?

Clarify the implementation assumptions: - With adjacency list + binary heap priority queue: O((V+E) log V) - With adjacency matrix + simple array min-scan: O(V^2) - With Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) amortized If forced to pick one, the most common practical answer is O((V+E) log V), but it’s best to mention the dependency on PQ choice.
I’ve also seen O(E log V + V log V) and O(E log V). Would you say O(E log V) is acceptable?
If you add a quick qualifier like “for connected graphs” or “since E is typically at least V,” then yes. Otherwise, safest is O((V+E) log V).
Can we dive into why decrease-key is O(log V) in a binary heap? I know bubble-up is log, but what about finding the element?

Good catch: a binary heap supports decrease-key in O(log V) only if you can locate the heap position of the element in O(1). So in Dijkstra: - You maintain an array pos[vertex] = index in heap array. - When you swap heap nodes during bubble-up/down, you update pos accordingly. Then decrease-key: - Look up index i = pos[v] in O(1) - Update key and bubble-up O(log V) Without that position map, finding v in the heap would be O(V), breaking the bound. In lazy duplicate approach, you avoid decrease-key entirely: you just insert a new pair (newDist, v), so no need to locate v. That’s why it’s popular.
That helps. So the textbook O((V+E) log V) with binary heap assumes we have decrease-key with a position map, right?

Exactly. Either: - A heap that supports decrease-key with handles/indices, or - A different PQ structure that supports decrease-key by design. Otherwise, you switch to lazy inserts and get O(E log E) style bounds.
Let’s do a concrete worst-case for lazy duplicates. Can we get Θ(E) pushes and Θ(E) pops?

Yes. Imagine a graph where many relaxations succeed: - Each time you find a better path to a vertex, you push a new entry. - In worst case, each edge relaxation could improve some vertex once, so pushes are Θ(E). Each push can eventually lead to a pop (either used or stale). So pops can be Θ(E). Each heap op is O(log heapSize) = O(log E). Thus Θ(E log E).
Is Θ(E log E) always equivalent to Θ(E log V)?

Not always as a strict function of two independent parameters, but often close. Since E can be as large as V^2 (simple directed graph without multi-edges is up to V(V−1)), log E ≤ log(V^2) = 2 log V, so log E = O(log V). Also, for connected sparse graphs, E is Ω(V), so log V = O(log E). So in many standard regimes, log E and log V differ by at most a constant factor, making E log E and E log V asymptotically equivalent up to constants. But if you allow E to be much smaller than V (like E = V), still log E and log V are Θ(log V). If E is extremely small compared to V (say E = 1), log E is constant but log V grows; then E log E and E log V differ. However, in that case the algorithm’s behavior also changes because you can’t reach many vertices from the source; using reachable subgraph sizes resolves the mismatch.
I want to practice explaining complexity cleanly. Can you give a structured explanation template?

A good template: 1) Specify representation and data structures: - adjacency list - min-priority queue implemented as binary heap with decrease-key (or lazy inserts) 2) Identify key operations and their costs: - extract-min: O(log V) - decrease-key/insert: O(log V) - scanning adjacency list: total O(E) 3) Count how many times each operation happens: - extract-min: ≤ V - decrease-key: ≤ E (successful relaxations) - insert: depends on variant 4) Multiply and sum: - V * log V + E * log V + E - simplify to O((V+E) log V) 5) Mention common simplifications/assumptions (connected graph → O(E log V)).
Nice. Now, could we also talk about space complexity for Dijkstra?

Space: - Graph storage adjacency list: O(V+E) - dist array: O(V) - parent/predecessor array: O(V) if you want paths - visited/settled boolean: O(V) - Priority queue: - decrease-key version: O(V) - lazy duplicates: O(E) in worst case due to duplicates So overall: - O(V+E) typical; lazy PQ can inflate heap memory to O(E).
In interview settings, is it okay to say O(V+E) space?
Yes, if you assume adjacency list and a PQ that holds at most one entry per vertex. If you mention lazy duplicates, note PQ can be O(E).
Let’s pivot to another complexity question: For Kruskal’s algorithm, why is it O(E log E)?

Kruskal: 1) Sort all edges by weight: O(E log E) 2) Iterate edges in order, union-find operations: - For each edge, do find(u), find(v), maybe union - With union by rank + path compression: almost O(1) amortized, formally O(α(V)) per op Total union-find cost: O(E α(V)). So sorting dominates: O(E log E). Since log E = O(log V^2) = O(log V), sometimes written O(E log V).
For Prim’s algorithm with a binary heap, what’s complexity?

Prim’s MST: - Similar structure to Dijkstra, but keys are min edge weight connecting to tree. - With adjacency list + binary heap decrease-key: - extract-min V times: O(V log V) - decrease-key up to E times: O(E log V) Total O((V+E) log V). With Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V).
So Dijkstra and Prim look basically identical in complexity with those structures.
Exactly. Both are “grow a set” algorithms using a PQ keyed by best-known connection value (distance or edge weight).
Another one: A* search complexity? People say it’s exponential in worst case. How does that relate to Dijkstra?

A* is Dijkstra with a modified priority: - Dijkstra prioritizes by g(n) = distance from source. - A* prioritizes by f(n) = g(n) + h(n) where h is heuristic estimate to goal. Worst-case, if h is poor (e.g., 0), A* degenerates to Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) for graph search with a PQ. If the state space is huge and not explicitly bounded as V/E, worst-case expansions can be exponential in solution depth (common in implicit graphs). So complexity depends on representation: - Explicit graph: same bounds as Dijkstra-like, up to scanning edges. - Implicit search tree: can be exponential due to branching.
That’s helpful. Now I want to drill Big-O manipulations. For example, if an algorithm is O(V log V + E), can we say O((V+E) log V)?

Not necessarily. O(V log V + E) is not always ≤ c (V+E) log V? Actually it is: - E ≤ E log V for V ≥ 2 (since log V ≥ 1), so E is O(E log V). So V log V + E is O(V log V + E log V) = O((V+E) log V). So yes, you can upper-bound it by O((V+E) log V), but it might be looser than necessary if E is small and log V large; still valid as an upper bound. Conversely, O((V+E) log V) implies O(V log V + E log V), which is not necessarily O(V log V + E) (because E log V could be larger than E).
So big-O can be made looser but still correct.
Right. In analysis, you often aim for a tight-ish bound, but any valid upper bound is technically correct.
Could you do a quick review of little-o, big-Theta, big-Omega?

Definitions (single-parameter n; extend similarly to multiple parameters): - Big-O: f(n) ∈ O(g(n)) if ∃ c, n0: f(n) ≤ c g(n) for n ≥ n0 (asymptotic upper bound). - Big-Ω: f(n) ∈ Ω(g(n)) if ∃ c, n0: f(n) ≥ c g(n) for n ≥ n0 (asymptotic lower bound). - Big-Θ: f(n) ∈ Θ(g(n)) if f(n) ∈ O(g(n)) and f(n) ∈ Ω(g(n)) (tight bound up to constants). - little-o: f(n) ∈ o(g(n)) if for all c>0, ∃ n0: f(n) < c g(n) for n ≥ n0 (strictly smaller order; equivalently lim f/g = 0). - little-ω: f(n) ∈ ω(g(n)) if lim f/g = ∞.
Great. Back to graphs: If we run Dijkstra from every vertex (all-pairs shortest paths), what’s complexity?

Running Dijkstra V times: - With binary heap: V * O((V+E) log V) = O(V(V+E) log V) = O((V^2 + VE) log V). For sparse graphs (E=O(V)): O(V^2 log V). For dense graphs (E=Θ(V^2)): O(V^3 log V). Alternatively: - Floyd–Warshall: O(V^3), good for dense graphs and simpler, but needs O(V^2) memory. - Johnson’s algorithm: reweights edges (Bellman-Ford once) then Dijkstra from each vertex: O(VE log V) (plus VE for reweighting), good for sparse graphs with negative edges but no negative cycles.
Johnson’s uses Dijkstra with reweighting. Complexity of Bellman-Ford part is O(VE), right?

Yes: - Add super-source, run Bellman-Ford: O(VE). - Then for each vertex, run Dijkstra: O(V * (E log V + V log V)) ≈ O(VE log V + V^2 log V). Usually written O(VE log V) for sparse connected graphs where E dominates V.
Another interview favorite: What’s the complexity of checking if a graph is bipartite?
Use BFS or DFS coloring: - Visit each vertex and edge once: O(V+E) - Space: O(V) for color array and queue/stack.
Let’s talk about complexity of union-find operations more precisely: α(V) is inverse Ackermann, basically constant. Is it fair to treat it as constant?
In practice and for typical theoretical discussions, yes—α(V) grows so slowly that for any realistic V it’s ≤ 4 or 5. But in formal asymptotic expressions, people keep α(V) to be precise.
Switching gears: Suppose we implement Dijkstra with a balanced BST (like TreeMap) instead of a heap. Complexity?

With a balanced BST keyed by distance: - extract-min: remove smallest key: O(log V) (find min could be O(1) if tree keeps leftmost pointer, but removal is log) - decrease-key: update key: remove old entry + insert new entry: O(log V) So asymptotically similar: O((V+E) log V). But duplicates and handling equal keys can complicate; heaps are usually simpler and faster.
I want to ensure I can explain why adjacency list scanning is O(E). Can you articulate that clearly?

In adjacency list representation: - Each vertex u has a list Adj[u] containing its outgoing neighbors (and weights). - The inner loop “for each edge (u,v) in Adj[u]” runs exactly len(Adj[u]) times for that u. - Summed over all vertices, total iterations = Σ_u len(Adj[u]) = E (for directed) or 2E (for undirected stored both ways). So total edge processing is linear in number of stored adjacency entries, i.e., O(E).
Perfect. Now, can we discuss worst-case for decrease-key count again? Is it really bounded by E even if distances can decrease multiple times?

Yes, because each decrease corresponds to some successful relaxation, and each successful relaxation corresponds to processing a specific edge once. Even if a vertex’s dist decreases many times, each decrease is “caused by” a different edge when it is scanned: - The algorithm only tries relaxations along edges it scans. - Each scan is a single event per edge. - A scan can trigger at most one decrease-key. So total decreases ≤ number of scans = E.
What about undirected graphs? Do we say E edges but store 2E adjacency entries, so does complexity become O((V+E) log V) or O((V+2E) log V)?

By convention, E is the number of edges in the mathematical graph. In adjacency lists for undirected graphs, you store each edge twice, so the number of adjacency entries is 2E. Complexity in terms of adjacency entries is O((V+2E) log V), which simplifies to O((V+E) log V) because constant factors are ignored.
If someone defines E as number of adjacency entries, then it’s still O((V+E) log V) with that definition.
Exactly. Just be consistent.
Let’s test understanding with an example graph and step through Dijkstra while tracking heap operations counts.

Consider directed graph with vertices {A,B,C,D} (V=4). Edges (E=5): A->B (1) A->C (4) B->C (2) B->D (5) C->D (1) Run Dijkstra from A. Initialize: dist[A]=0, dist[others]=∞. Heap contains A,B,C,D in decrease-key model. Operations so far: build heap O(V). Loop: 1) extract-min => A (1 op) Scan A edges: - relax A->B: dist[B]=1, decrease-key(B) (1 op) - relax A->C: dist[C]=4, decrease-key(C) (1 op) 2) extract-min => B (1 op) Scan B edges: - relax B->C: dist[C]=min(4,1+2=3)=3, decrease-key(C) (1 op) - relax B->D: dist[D]=6, decrease-key(D) (1 op) 3) extract-min => C (1 op) Scan C edges: - relax C->D: dist[D]=min(6,3+1=4)=4, decrease-key(D) (1 op) 4) extract-min => D (1 op) Scan D edges: none. Counts: - extract-min: 4 = V - decrease-key: 5 successful relaxations? Actually successes: A->B, A->C, B->C, B->D, C->D = 5 = E (all edges succeeded here) So heap operations: V extracts + E decreases = 9 operations, each O(log V).
Great. In this example all edges relaxed successfully. That’s why decrease-key count hits E.
Exactly.
Now, can we discuss complexity of Dijkstra on a grid graph, like for pathfinding? E is roughly 4V. So complexity is O(V log V) basically?

Yes. In a 2D grid with 4-neighborhood: - E ≈ 2*(rows*(cols-1) + cols*(rows-1)) ≈ 2V*something, but Θ(V). So (V+E) log V = Θ(V log V). That’s why Dijkstra can be somewhat heavy on large grids; A* with a good heuristic can reduce explored nodes.
I also see people say Dijkstra is “O(E log V)” and for grids that’s O(V log V). Consistent.
Right.
Another thing: if weights are small integers, there’s Dial’s algorithm with buckets. Complexity?

Dial’s algorithm (bucket-based priority queue) works when edge weights are nonnegative integers bounded by C. - It uses an array of buckets for distance mod something (or up to max distance). - Complexity: O(E + V*C) or O(E + W*V) depending on details; more precisely O(E + V * C) when distances don’t exceed VC in worst case, but the typical statement is O(E + V*C) for bounded integer weights with appropriate bucket structure. There’s also radix heap giving O((V+E) log C) or similar. The takeaway: - If weights are small integers, you can do better than log V factors.
For 0-1 weights we already saw O(V+E). That’s a special case of buckets with C=1.
Exactly.
Let’s talk about complexity pitfalls. Sometimes someone says Dijkstra is O(E log E) with a heap. Is that wrong?

Not wrong—just depends on the heap usage pattern. - With lazy duplicates: O(E log E) - With decrease-key and one heap entry per vertex: O((V+E) log V) Many implementations in languages without decrease-key use the lazy approach, so O(E log E) is a fair bound to cite for that code.
In that lazy approach, do we still need a visited set?

Usually yes (or at least a settled boolean) to avoid re-processing a vertex’s outgoing edges multiple times. Common patterns: - Option 1: If you pop (d,u) and if u is already settled, skip. - Option 2: If d != dist[u], skip. Then when you process u, you can mark it settled. Both help ensure each vertex’s adjacency list is scanned once, keeping edge scanning O(E).
Without visited/settled, we might scan edges multiple times and blow up complexity.
Exactly. If you re-process adjacency lists for stale pops, total scans could become large (potentially O(E^2) in contrived cases), so you typically guard against that.
Can we analyze that? Suppose you don’t mark settled and you process every pop. What happens?

Then each time a stale entry for u is popped, you’d scan Adj[u] again, re-relax edges, possibly pushing more entries. In worst-case graphs, this can cascade. The clean complexity bound relies on scanning each adjacency list once (settled once), so you need the settled check (or equivalent) to prevent repeated scans.
Good to know. Now, could you summarize common time complexities for shortest path variants?

Single-source: - Unweighted: BFS O(V+E) - 0-1 weights: 0-1 BFS O(V+E) - Nonnegative weights: - Dijkstra + binary heap: O((V+E) log V) - Dijkstra + Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) - Dijkstra + buckets (small integer weights): O(E + VC) (Dial) - Negative weights (no negative cycles): Bellman-Ford O(VE) - DAG shortest paths (can have negative weights, no cycles): - Topological order + relax edges: O(V+E) All-pairs: - Floyd–Warshall: O(V^3) - Johnson: O(VE log V) (plus VE)
DAG shortest path O(V+E) is interesting. Why can it handle negative weights?

Because there are no cycles, so you can process vertices in topological order. When you relax edges in that order, each edge is relaxed once and no later vertex can lead back to earlier ones. Negative weights don’t cause the “settle too early” issue because the acyclic structure prevents revisiting dependencies.
That’s a nice contrast with Dijkstra’s reliance on nonnegative weights.
Exactly.

Now I want to practice deriving complexity of a new algorithm. Suppose an algorithm does: - For each vertex, it does a heap insert O(log V) - For each edge, it does a constant amount of work - Then it does V times extract-min O(log V) Total is O(V log V + E + V log V) = O((V log V) + E). Could we simplify?
