Yes: Total = O(2V log V + E) = O(V log V + E). You can’t simplify to O((V+E) log V) without making it looser, but you could if you want an upper bound: O(V log V + E) ⊆ O((V+E) log V) since E ≤ E log V for V≥2. But the tighter expression is O(E + V log V).
That looks like Fibonacci heap Dijkstra form.
Right—same algebraic structure.
Let’s return to Dijkstra and talk about the effect of directed vs undirected.

Complexity is the same form, but E counts directed edges: - Directed graph: adjacency list entries = E. - Undirected: entries = 2E if stored both ways, but still O(E). So O((V+E) log V) in either case, with E interpreted consistently.
Another subtlety: In some proofs, they say extract-min happens V times, but what if graph is disconnected?

If you initialize PQ with all vertices and run the classic version, you still do V extract-min operations, but vertices not reachable from source will have dist = ∞ and will be extracted eventually. If you instead initialize PQ with only the source and insert discovered vertices, then you do extract-min only for reachable vertices Vr and scan only edges Er in the reachable subgraph. Complexity becomes O((Vr+Er) log Vr).
That’s a good nuance. Now, could we talk about the difference between worst-case and average-case in these analyses?

Most of these are worst-case bounds. Average-case depends on distribution of graphs and weights, which is rarely specified. For interview and algorithm analysis, you typically provide worst-case. Some data structures (like hash tables) have average-case O(1) operations but worst-case O(n). Heaps have deterministic worst-case O(log n) for the operations we discussed.
For Dijkstra, are there any probabilistic improvements?

There are specialized priority queues and heuristic approaches, but the classic theoretical improvements are more about amortized structures (Fibonacci, radix heaps) and constraints on weights (integer bounded). There are also advanced algorithms for planar graphs or road networks using preprocessing (contraction hierarchies, etc.), but that’s beyond the standard bound discussion.
Let’s focus on Big-O. Another question: if we say Dijkstra is O((V+E) log V), is that worst-case with respect to what? Input size?

It’s with respect to the parameters V and E of the input graph. If you define input size as number of vertices plus edges (plus weights), then it’s polynomial-time in input size, but the log V factor makes it slightly superlinear in E for sparse graphs. If you want to be very formal about bit complexity, weights and arithmetic operations matter; typically we assume constant-time arithmetic on weights (like machine integers) and focus on combinatorial complexity.
Bit complexity is probably too deep for now. But good to know.
Agreed.
Another manipulation: For dense graphs, E = Θ(V^2). Then Dijkstra with heap is O(V^2 log V). But matrix-based is O(V^2). So matrix wins asymptotically.
Correct. For dense graphs, the O(V^2) implementation can be better. That’s why the “best” Dijkstra implementation depends on density.
Could we quantify the crossover? Like when does V^2 beat E log V?

Compare: - Heap: (V+E) log V ~ E log V (if E dominates) - Matrix: V^2 Matrix better when V^2 < E log V? Actually matrix is V^2, heap is E log V. Matrix wins when: V^2 = o(E log V) is false; we want V^2 < E log V means heap worse. But since E ≤ V^2, E log V ≤ V^2 log V. For dense E ~ V^2, heap is V^2 log V > V^2, so matrix wins. For sparse E ~ V, heap is V log V < V^2, heap wins. Rough crossover when E log V ≈ V^2 ⇒ E ≈ V^2 / log V.
Nice. Now, I’d like to practice explaining why Dijkstra requires nonnegative weights with a counterexample.

Simple counterexample: Vertices: S, A, B Edges: S -> A (2) S -> B (1) A -> B (-5) Shortest path from S to B is S->A->B with cost 2 + (-5) = -3. Run Dijkstra: - Initialize dist[S]=0, dist[A]=2, dist[B]=1 - Extract-min: B (distance 1) because it’s smallest. - Dijkstra would finalize B with distance 1. But later when A is extracted, relaxing A->B would produce -3, contradicting finalization. So Dijkstra fails with negative edges because the “greedy” settle step is invalid.
Great. Now, can we talk about complexity of Bellman-Ford and why it’s VE?

Bellman-Ford: - Initialize dist - Repeat V−1 times: - For each edge (u,v,w): - relax: dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u]+w) Each pass scans all E edges: O(E) Number of passes: V−1: O(V) Total: O(VE) Why V−1 passes? The shortest path in a graph without negative cycles can have at most V−1 edges (simple path). Each pass can propagate improvements one edge further.
Good. Now, let’s go to complexity of detecting negative cycles reachable from source.
After V−1 passes, do one more pass over all edges: - If any dist[v] can still be decreased, then there is a negative cycle reachable from source. That extra pass is O(E). Total remains O(VE).
Back to Dijkstra: does it compute shortest paths tree? Complexity to reconstruct path?

If you store parent[v] when you relax successfully, you can reconstruct the shortest path to any vertex by following parents backward. Path reconstruction time is O(length of path), at most O(V). This doesn’t change the main algorithm complexity.
Now, I want to get better at explaining “amortized.” Could you explain amortized analysis in context of Fibonacci heap Dijkstra?

Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over a sequence, without assuming randomness. Some operations may be expensive occasionally, but the total over many operations is bounded. In Fibonacci heap: - decrease-key is O(1) amortized because it does limited restructuring per decrease, deferring more expensive consolidations. - extract-min does consolidation of trees; that’s O(log V) amortized. In Dijkstra: - There are many decrease-keys (up to E). With O(1) amortized each, total O(E). - Extract-min happens V times with O(log V) amortized each, total O(V log V). So overall O(E + V log V) amortized.
Okay. Another question: For binary heap, are those worst-case or amortized?
Binary heap operations insert, extract-min, decrease-key are worst-case O(log V) each (deterministic).
Good. Now, can we talk about complexity of building the heap initially? Is it O(V) or O(V log V)?

If you have all V keys in an array, you can build a binary heap in O(V) using heapify (bottom-up sift-down). If you insert V items one by one, it’s O(V log V). In Dijkstra, either is acceptable. The main term often already includes V log V, so it doesn’t change asymptotics.
If we insert only the source initially, then heap build cost is O(1) and we insert other vertices as discovered.
Yes.
Now I’d like to discuss algorithm complexity notation with multiple variables: sometimes people write O(E log V + V log V). Is it okay to factor log V?
Yes: O(E log V + V log V) = O((E+V) log V). Factoring is just algebra.
Another: O(E log V + V) simplifies to O(E log V + V). Can we drop V?

Only if you assume E log V dominates V. If E is at least 1 and V is large, log V can be large, but E could be 0. Without assumptions, keep both. If graph is connected and V≥2, E≥V−1, then E log V ≥ (V−1) log V, which dominates V for sufficiently large V, so you could drop V. But you’d need that assumption.
Understood. Can we do a quick list of typical complexities for heap operations?

Binary heap: - find-min: O(1) - insert: O(log n) - extract-min: O(log n) - decrease-key: O(log n) if position known - delete: O(log n) if position known - build-heap: O(n) Fibonacci heap (amortized): - find-min: O(1) - insert: O(1) - decrease-key: O(1) - merge: O(1) - extract-min: O(log n)
That’s helpful. Now, can we discuss time complexity of “relaxation” itself? It’s constant, right?
Yes, assuming constant-time arithmetic and comparisons: - compute dist[u] + w - compare to dist[v] - maybe assign and update parent So O(1) per edge scan, plus PQ update cost when successful.
Great. Now: In Dijkstra, do we relax edges from u even if u’s dist is infinity?

If you include unreachable vertices in the PQ (initially all vertices), eventually you might extract a vertex with dist=∞. At that point, all remaining vertices in PQ also have dist=∞ (since ∞ is minimum). You could stop early because no further improvements are possible. Many implementations break when extract-min returns ∞. If you don’t break, scanning edges from an unreachable vertex is wasted work. Early stopping can reduce work in disconnected graphs.
Complexity-wise, early stopping changes from O((V+E) log V) to O((Vr+Er) log Vr) for reachable part.
Exactly.
Now, I want to test reasoning: If Dijkstra uses adjacency matrix and a heap, would that help?

Not much, because with adjacency matrix: - Even after extracting u, to relax outgoing edges you must scan all V possible neighbors to see if there’s an edge: O(V) per extracted vertex. So edge relaxation scanning becomes O(V^2) regardless. The heap would reduce min-selection from O(V) to O(log V), but total becomes: - V extract-min: O(V log V) - V iterations scanning V neighbors: O(V^2) Total O(V^2 + V log V) = O(V^2). So adjacency matrix dominates.
So the heap advantage relies on adjacency lists to avoid scanning non-edges.
Exactly.
Another question: If we want shortest path in an unweighted graph, BFS is O(V+E). Is there a way to see BFS as Dijkstra with a special PQ?

Yes. Dijkstra with all weights = 1: - Distances increase in integer steps. - The min-priority queue always extracts vertices in nondecreasing distance. BFS with a FIFO queue achieves the same because when all edges have equal weight, the first time you discover a vertex is via the shortest path, and the discovery order by layers matches nondecreasing distance. So BFS is Dijkstra specialized to unit weights with an O(1) queue instead of a PQ.
Nice. Could we now talk about complexity of graph representation conversions? Like building adjacency list from edge list.

Given an edge list of size E: - Initialize array of lists of size V: O(V) - For each edge (u,v,w), append to Adj[u] (and Adj[v] if undirected): O(1) amortized per append Total O(V+E) time and O(V+E) space.
Great. Now I want to practice a more formal proof style for Dijkstra complexity. Can you write it out in a more “proof-like” way?

Sure. Assume: - Graph G=(V,E) stored as adjacency lists. - Priority queue is a binary heap with operations: extract-min in O(log |V|), decrease-key in O(log |V|). - Each vertex has a handle/position in heap to support decrease-key. Claim: Dijkstra runs in O((|V|+|E|) log |V|). Proof: Initialization: - dist initialized for all vertices: O(|V|). - heap constructed over |V| elements: O(|V|) via heapify (or O(|V| log |V|) via inserts; either is within O(|V| log |V|)). Main loop: - Each iteration performs one extract-min. Since each iteration removes one vertex from the heap, there are at most |V| iterations. Total time for all extract-min operations is O(|V| log |V|). - For each extracted vertex u, the algorithm scans all edges outgoing from u, i.e., iterates over Adj[u]. Over the entire run, each adjacency list is scanned once, so total scans is Σ_u |Adj[u]| = |E|. Each scan does O(1) work plus possibly a decrease-key. - A decrease-key is executed only when an edge relaxation strictly decreases dist[v]. Each successful relaxation is associated with a distinct edge scan event, and there are |E| edge scan events. Therefore there are at most |E| decrease-key operations. Total time for decrease-keys is O(|E| log |V|). Summing: O(|V|) + O(|V| log |V|) + O(|E|) + O(|E| log |V|) = O((|V|+|E|) log |V|). QED.
That’s the structure I wanted. Now, can we talk about why extract-min is exactly V times in that proof? It’s because each iteration removes one vertex from heap.

Correct. In the decrease-key model, heap initially contains all vertices (or all reachable ones inserted once). Each extract-min removes one vertex permanently. You can’t remove more than V vertices, so extract-min count is ≤ V.
If we insert only source and then insert vertices when found, extract-min count is number of inserted vertices, which is <= V.
Exactly.
Now, what about edge cases with parallel edges or self-loops? Do they affect complexity?

Complexity in terms of E counts them: - Parallel edges increase E, so scanning increases proportionally. - Self-loops are edges too; scanning them is constant work. So bound stays O((V+E) log V). Correctness is unaffected by self-loops with nonnegative weights; parallel edges are fine too.
Another thing: Sometimes graphs are weighted but weights can be zero. Dijkstra still works, right?
Yes. Dijkstra requires nonnegative weights, and zero is allowed.
All right. I want to move into algorithm complexity of strongly connected components (SCC) algorithms: Kosaraju and Tarjan.

Both are linear-time: Kosaraju: - DFS on G to compute finishing times: O(V+E) - Transpose graph: O(V+E) - DFS on transpose in decreasing finishing time order: O(V+E) Total O(V+E). Tarjan: - Single DFS with stack and lowlink values: O(V+E).
And space O(V+E) due to graph storage and recursion stack.
Yes; plus O(V) for arrays and stack.
Great. Now, I want to make sure I can compare complexities with different input sizes. For example, if E is huge, O(E log V) vs O(V^2) comparisons.

Key is to express everything in V and E and then substitute regimes: - Sparse: E = O(V) → O((V+E) log V) = O(V log V) - Dense: E = Θ(V^2) → O((V+E) log V) = O(V^2 log V) Compare to O(V^2) matrix approach: matrix better for dense.
All right. Let’s talk about complexity of computing transitive closure. Floyd-Warshall is O(V^3). But using repeated BFS/DFS from each vertex is O(V(V+E)).

Correct. - For unweighted reachability, run BFS/DFS from each vertex: O(V(V+E)). If E is sparse (O(V)), that’s O(V^2). If E is dense (Θ(V^2)), that’s O(V^3), same as Floyd-Warshall. Floyd-Warshall is simpler for dense graphs and yields all pairs distances for weighted graphs (with no negative cycles) in O(V^3).
Another: Complexity of finding bridges and articulation points?
Using DFS with low-link values: - Time O(V+E) - Space O(V)
Great. Now, I want to return again to Dijkstra to ensure I can handle tricky questions. Suppose we implement Dijkstra with a max-heap by storing negative distances. Complexity same?
Yes, if you negate keys consistently. It’s still a heap with log-time operations.
Another tricky: If we use a priority queue that supports delete-min in O(1) but decrease-key in O(V), what happens?
Then Dijkstra could degrade: - decrease-key called up to E times, each O(V) → O(EV), which is too slow. So you need decrease-key efficient (or avoid it via lazy inserts).
That’s a good point. The PQ operation mix matters.
Exactly.
Can we discuss the complexity of the “visited” array check? It’s O(1) each time, so negligible.
Right.
I’d like to practice another derivation: Why is BFS O(V+E)? Could you do a formal counting similar to Dijkstra?

Sure. BFS: - Initialize visited array: O(V) - Enqueue start vertex: O(1) - While queue not empty: - Dequeue u: each vertex dequeued at most once → O(V) total dequeues - For each neighbor v in Adj[u]: - Check visited[v] and maybe enqueue: each edge (u,v) considered once → O(E) total neighbor iterations Queue operations are O(1). So total O(V+E).
Great. Now, could you explain why DFS is also O(V+E) even though recursion might revisit nodes?
DFS marks visited when first entering a vertex, and only recurses on unvisited neighbors. So each vertex is entered once, each adjacency list is scanned once, total O(V+E).
All right. Now, can we talk about complexity of cycle detection in directed graphs?
Using DFS with recursion stack (colors: white/gray/black): - Time O(V+E) - Space O(V)
Good. Now, I want to discuss complexity of shortest path in DAG again. Could you show the algorithm?

Algorithm: 1) Topologically sort vertices: O(V+E) 2) Initialize dist[source]=0, others=∞ 3) Process vertices in topo order: for each edge (u,v,w): relax: dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u] + w) Each edge relaxed once: O(E) Total O(V+E).
Great. Now: complexity of K shortest paths? That can get complicated; maybe skip.
It varies widely (Yen’s algorithm, Eppstein’s), and bounds depend on K and data structures. Probably best to leave it unless you want to focus on a specific algorithm.
Let’s stick to fundamentals. Another question: Could you explain why Big-O uses worst-case and not best-case?

Big-O is an upper bound, often used to characterize worst-case because it guarantees performance regardless of input. Best-case can be misleading if it occurs rarely. You can analyze best-case too, but worst-case is the typical default.
Average-case is also useful but requires assumptions.
Exactly.
Now, can we talk about time complexity of sorting and its role in graph algorithms like Kruskal?

Sorting E edges dominates many MST and graph processing tasks: - Comparison sort lower bound is Ω(E log E) comparisons. So Kruskal is essentially optimal up to union-find overhead in comparison model. If weights are integers in bounded range, you can do counting/radix sort to reduce sorting time, improving Kruskal accordingly.
That’s similar to bucket Dijkstra improvements with bounded weights.
Yes—bounded integer keys often enable linear-ish time.
Another graph algorithm: shortest path in unweighted graph between two nodes can stop early when target is reached. Complexity becomes O(V+E) still worst-case but better in practice.
Right. Worst-case still O(V+E) if target is far or unreachable, but early exit can reduce explored region.
Dijkstra also can stop when target is settled.
Yes. If you only need shortest path to a target t, you can stop when t is extracted (settled). Complexity depends on explored subgraph.
But worst-case it still explores all vertices and edges.
Correct.
I’d like to ask about complexity of using adjacency lists vs edge lists directly in Dijkstra.

If you store graph as an edge list only, to relax edges out of u you’d need to scan all E edges to find those with source u, which would be O(E) per extracted vertex → O(VE). So adjacency lists (or some indexing) are important for efficiency.
Good. Now, can we talk about memory locality? Binary heap is cache-friendly, Fibonacci heap not so much. That’s why binary heap is faster in practice.
Exactly. Big-O doesn’t capture constants and memory access patterns. Binary heaps are array-based and have good locality; Fibonacci heaps are pointer-heavy with more overhead.
Now I want to practice a typical interview follow-up: “Why not use BFS for weighted graphs?” Answer: BFS assumes uniform weights.
Right. BFS explores by number of edges, not total weight. With varying weights, fewer edges doesn’t mean lower cost, so BFS can be wrong.
Another follow-up: “Can Dijkstra handle negative edges if there are no negative cycles?” No.
Correct. Even a single negative edge can break it, as shown.
Another: “What if weights are all positive but floating point?” Dijkstra still works as long as comparisons and additions behave; complexity same.
Yes, assuming constant-time arithmetic. Beware of floating-point precision in practice, but algorithmic complexity unchanged.
Now, I want to go deeper into the formal reason extract-min gives final distances: Could you provide the key lemma?

Lemma (Dijkstra correctness): When a vertex u is extracted (has minimum tentative distance among unsettled vertices), dist[u] equals the true shortest path distance δ(s,u). Proof sketch: Assume for contradiction there is a shorter path P from s to u than dist[u] at extraction. Let x be the first vertex on P not yet settled at extraction time, and let y be its predecessor on P (so y is settled). When y was settled, edge (y,x) was relaxed, so dist[x] ≤ dist[y] + w(y,x) = δ(s,y) + w(y,x) = δ(s,x). Thus dist[x] ≤ δ(s,x). Since weights are nonnegative, δ(s,x) ≤ δ(s,u) < dist[u]. So dist[x] < dist[u], contradicting that u had minimum tentative distance among unsettled vertices. Therefore dist[u] is optimal.
That’s clear. Complexity relies on each vertex settled once.
Exactly.
Now, I’d like to discuss complexity of implementing the PQ with an array and scanning for min each time. That’s O(V^2 + E) with adjacency list, right?
Yes: - V times find-min by scanning all vertices: O(V^2) - Edge scans: O(E) Total O(V^2 + E) = O(V^2).
Good. Now, can we compare that to using a heap in sparse graphs?
Sparse graphs E=O(V): - Array-scan: O(V^2) - Heap: O(V log V) Heap is asymptotically better.
Now, I want to test algebra: If E=V log V, then heap Dijkstra is O((V+V log V) log V)=O(V log^2 V). Array-scan is O(V^2). Heap still better.
Correct.
Good. Now, can we talk about complexity of “decrease-key” calls in Prim’s algorithm? Similar to Dijkstra, up to E.
Yes. Each edge can improve the best known connection to the MST at most once when scanned; total decrease-key ≤ E.

Great. Now, could we talk about the complexity of using adjacency list with a Fibonacci heap but without decrease-key handles? That doesn’t make sense; Fibonacci heap decrease-key needs node pointers.
Correct. Fibonacci heap’s decrease-key requires a pointer/handle to the heap node. In algorithms, you maintain an array mapping vertex -> heap node pointer.
Same idea as pos[] for binary heap.
Exactly.
Now, I want to cover complexity of other priority-queue-based algorithms: Huffman coding is O(n log n) because of n extracts and inserts.
Yes: - Build min-heap of n frequencies: O(n) - Repeat n−1 times: extract two mins (2 log n) and insert combined (log n) Total O(n log n).
Great. Now, another: event simulation with PQ is O(m log m) where m events.
Correct.
Coming back: Another graph algorithm with PQ is “best-first search.” Complexity similar patterns.
Yes—depends on number of pushes/pops and log of PQ size.
I think I’ve got Dijkstra complexity now. Could you give me a compact explanation I can recite?

Dijkstra with adjacency lists and a binary heap: - Each vertex is extracted once: V extract-min operations, each O(log V) → O(V log V). - Each edge is examined once during relaxation: O(E). - Each successful relaxation causes a decrease-key, at most once per edge: ≤ E decrease-keys, each O(log V) → O(E log V). Total O((V+E) log V), space O(V+E).
Perfect. Now I’ll ask a few rapid-fire complexity questions to reinforce.
Go ahead.
Complexity of BFS?
O(V+E).
Complexity of DFS?
O(V+E).
Complexity of topological sort?
O(V+E).
Complexity of Bellman-Ford?
O(VE).
Complexity of Floyd-Warshall?
Complexity of Kruskal?
O(E log E) (plus nearly linear union-find).
Complexity of Prim with binary heap?
O((V+E) log V).
Great. Now I want to end by asking: what are the most common mistakes people make when stating Dijkstra complexity?

Common mistakes: 1) Saying O(E log V) without clarifying assumptions (connected/reachable subgraph). 2) Forgetting that decrease-key needs a handle/position map; otherwise complexity changes. 3) Mixing adjacency matrix with heap and still claiming O(E log V); matrix implies O(V^2) scanning. 4) Ignoring that lazy duplicate implementation yields O(E log E) heap ops and possibly O(E) PQ space. 5) Claiming Dijkstra works with negative edges.
That wraps it up. Could you also list a few practice prompts I can use to self-check complexity reasoning?

Prompts: - Derive Dijkstra complexity under (a) adjacency matrix + array scan, (b) adjacency list + binary heap, (c) adjacency list + lazy heap duplicates, (d) Fibonacci heap. - For a graph with E = V−1, compare Dijkstra heap vs array-scan. - For E = V^2, compare matrix vs heap. - Prove BFS is O(V+E) by counting queue ops and edge scans. - Given an algorithm that does one heap op per edge and one per vertex, express time complexity and simplify under sparse/dense regimes.
Thanks.
I keep getting a RecursionError in my tree traversal. Can you help me figure out why?

Yes. A RecursionError in a tree traversal usually means one of these is happening: (1) the structure isn’t actually a tree (it has cycles or shared references that create a loop), (2) your base case never triggers for some nodes, (3) you’re accidentally recursing into the same node repeatedly (e.g., wrong child pointer, parent link), or (4) the tree is extremely deep and exceeds Python’s recursion limit. Can you share the traversal code and a small example of how the nodes are built?
Sure, here’s a simplified version. It’s supposed to be a DFS preorder traversal:

class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val self.left = None self.right = None def preorder(node, out): if not node: return out.append(node.val) preorder(node.left, out) preorder(node.right, out) But in my real code, Node has a parent pointer too, and I think that’s where things get messy. Sometimes it blows up.

The shown preorder is fine for a strict acyclic tree. If your real Node has a parent pointer, the most common failure mode is accidentally traversing the parent as if it were a child (directly or indirectly), creating an infinite loop: node -> parent -> child -> parent ... Another common issue is that some “child” pointer is mistakenly set to the parent or to itself. Can you show the real Node class and the traversal you run in the failing case? Also, how do you construct the nodes—any helper that sets parent/child links?
Real code is more like this:

class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val self.left = None self.right = None self.parent = None def connect(parent, left=None, right=None): parent.left = left parent.right = right if left: left.parent = parent if right: right.parent = parent return parent def walk(node, out): if node is None: return out.append(node.val) if node.left: walk(node.left, out) if node.right: walk(node.right, out) if node.parent: walk(node.parent, out) I added the parent walk because I wanted to traverse “all connected nodes” from an arbitrary starting node, not necessarily the root. But I think this causes recursion error.

Yes, that will recurse forever for any node that has both a parent and at least one child somewhere in the chain. You’ve turned the tree into an undirected graph by adding edges upward, and your traversal is a graph traversal without visited tracking. Example: start at a node with a parent; you visit node, then parent, then parent’s left/right, which includes your original node again, etc. To traverse “all connected nodes” in a structure with parent pointers, you need to avoid revisiting nodes. Two common fixes: 1) Track a visited set of node identities (e.g., id(node) or node object itself if hashable). 2) Pass the previous node (the node you came from) and avoid stepping back. Simplest: def walk(node, out, visited=None): if node is None: return if visited is None: visited = set() if node in visited: return visited.add(node) out.append(node.val) walk(node.left, out, visited) walk(node.right, out, visited) walk(node.parent, out, visited) But Node instances are hashable by default only if you don’t override __eq__. Do you override __eq__ or make Node a dataclass with eq=True? If so, using node in a set might not work as intended.
I actually made it a dataclass in the real project:
from dataclasses import dataclass @dataclass class Node: val: int left: "Node" = None right: "Node" = None parent: "Node" = None So I think it generates __eq__ automatically.

Right. With a dataclass default (eq=True, frozen=False), it generates __eq__ and sets __hash__ = None, meaning instances become unhashable. So you can’t put Node objects into a set directly. Two easy approaches: A) Use visited as a set of ids: visited_ids = set() if id(node) in visited_ids: return visited_ids.add(id(node)) B) Make the dataclass hashable by identity: set eq=False (so equality is identity), or define __hash__ = object.__hash__. Option B is often clean: @dataclass(eq=False) class Node: val: int left: "Node" = None right: "Node" = None parent: "Node" = None Then you can store nodes in a set and it’ll use identity-based hashing. Which approach fits your design? If you rely on structural equality of nodes somewhere else, then use id-based visited instead.
I don’t rely on structural equality. I just used dataclass for convenience. So eq=False seems fine. But I also worry about memory leaks or keeping nodes alive via visited. Is that an issue?

The visited set will keep strong references for the duration of the traversal call, but once the function returns, the set is eligible for garbage collection. So no leak in the typical sense. If you store visited globally or return it, then it would keep nodes alive. If you want to be extra cautious for long-lived caches, you can use weak references via weakref.WeakSet, but for a traversal function, a local set is normal. So: set eq=False, then implement visited. Also: consider whether you want to include parent edges at all. If your goal is “all connected nodes” from an arbitrary node, you’re doing graph traversal (DFS/BFS). Parent pointers just add edges; visited is mandatory.
That makes sense. But I tried a visited set with ids and still got RecursionError sometimes. Here’s what I did:

def walk(node, out, visited=None): if visited is None: visited = set() if node is None: return if id(node) in visited: return visited.add(id(node)) out.append(node.val) if node.left: walk(node.left, out, visited) if node.right: walk(node.right, out, visited) if node.parent: walk(node.parent, out, visited) And it still overflowed on a big structure.

If visited is correct, infinite recursion due to cycles should stop. So remaining causes: 1) The structure is extremely deep (a chain), so even without cycles, recursion depth exceeds the Python recursion limit (~1000 by default). 2) There’s a bug where you create many distinct Node objects in a chain via lazy construction or property access, so visited never repeats. 3) You accidentally pass a fresh visited set in recursive calls in the real code (but your snippet passes it correctly). 4) You have recursion elsewhere (like properties that compute children recursively). Let’s distinguish depth vs. cycle. How many nodes are in the worst case, and what’s the shape? Is it more like a linked list (degenerate tree)? Also, can you show the traceback line count or the last few frames? If it repeats the same lines without repeating node ids, it’s likely depth.
It’s basically a parse tree for expressions, and some expressions can be nested thousands deep (generated input). So it can be a long chain. The traceback repeats walk(node.left...) a lot.

Then it’s depth. Python recursion isn’t suitable for arbitrary-depth trees; you’ll hit recursion limits and also risk C stack overflow if you raise it too far. Solution: rewrite traversal iteratively using an explicit stack (list/deque). For graph traversal with parent edges, still track visited. Example iterative DFS: def walk_iter(start): out = [] visited = set() stack = [start] while stack: node = stack.pop() if node is None: continue nid = id(node) if nid in visited: continue visited.add(nid) out.append(node.val) # push neighbors in reverse desired order because stack is LIFO # For preorder-like: node, left, right, parent (or any order) if node.parent: stack.append(node.parent) if node.right: stack.append(node.right) if node.left: stack.append(node.left) return out This avoids recursion depth issues. If you want exact ordering (preorder from a root), you wouldn’t include parent edges. But for “connected nodes” from arbitrary start, ordering is less canonical; define neighbor order. Would you like BFS instead (queue), or does DFS order matter?

Order doesn’t matter much, but I do want deterministic output for testing. DFS is fine. But I also want to avoid repeatedly visiting parent then children causing big overhead. Is there a better approach?

With visited, each node is processed once, so overhead is O(V + E). In your structure, each node has up to 3 edges (left, right, parent), so it’s linear. However, the neighbor order can affect how quickly you reach the whole component, but not total complexity. Determinism: ensure you always push neighbors in a fixed order. For DFS using a stack, the actual visit order depends on push order. If you want stable order: define neighbors as [left, right, parent] and push in reverse: parent, right, left. Also, if your tree can have shared subtrees (DAG), visited will prevent repeated processing. If you’re worried about overhead from parent edges specifically and you only need to traverse the subtree rooted at some node but not go up, then remove parent edge and just traverse children. But you said you want all connected nodes from arbitrary node, so parent edges are necessary to reach ancestors and siblings.

I think connected nodes is correct. But I’m realizing that in a pure tree, “connected component” is the whole tree anyway. Starting from a leaf, I want to traverse entire tree. So yes, parent traversal is needed.

Exactly. That’s basically treating the tree as an undirected connected graph and traversing from a starting node. One more detail: if you want “entire tree” from any node, you could first climb to the root (follow parent pointers until parent is None) and then do a normal preorder from root without parent edges. That would give a canonical tree traversal order and avoid visited overhead. Algorithm: def root_of(node): while node and node.parent: node = node.parent return node def preorder_iter(root): out=[] stack=[root] while stack: n=stack.pop() if n is None: continue out.append(n.val) if n.right: stack.append(n.right) if n.left: stack.append(n.left) return out This assumes parent pointers form a proper tree (no cycles upwards) and that you truly have a single root. If your structure might not be a strict tree (e.g., multiple parents, cycles), then stick with visited graph traversal. Does your parse tree guarantee a single root and correct parent pointers?
Yes, there’s a single root. Parent pointers should be consistent. No DAG sharing. So climbing to root seems better.

Then I’d do that: find root, then traverse children only. That avoids recursion and avoids visited. Complexity is linear, and order is deterministic. Potential pitfall: if there’s a bug where parent pointers contain a cycle (node.parent eventually loops), root_of would loop forever. You can protect with visited ids while climbing: def root_of(node): seen=set() while node and node.parent: nid=id(node) if nid in seen: raise ValueError("Cycle in parent pointers") seen.add(nid) node=node.parent return node Then traverse.

Great. But I still have one place where I really do need to walk “all neighbors” because there are extra edges besides parent/child (like cross-links for symbol references). That’s a graph. How would you structure that traversal cleanly?

For a general graph, model neighbors explicitly and do iterative DFS/BFS with visited. If Node has multiple kinds of edges, provide a generator: def neighbors(node): if node.left: yield node.left if node.right: yield node.right if node.parent: yield node.parent for ref in node.refs:  # e.g. list of nodes if ref: yield ref Then: def dfs(start): out=[] visited=set() stack=[start] while stack: node=stack.pop() if node is None: continue nid=id(node) if nid in visited: continue visited.add(nid) out.append(node.val) # deterministic: gather neighbors in a list, then push reversed nbrs=list(neighbors(node)) for nxt in reversed(nbrs): stack.append(nxt) return out You can also separate “visit action” from traversal by accepting a callback. If you need topological-like ordering, that’s different and requires DAG assumptions.
I implemented something like that, but I noticed my output order changes depending on insertion order of refs, which can vary. For tests, I want stable output even if refs are stored in a set.

If refs is a set, iteration order can vary across runs (especially with hash randomization). For deterministic traversal, you must impose an order when expanding neighbors. Options: - Store refs in a list in deterministic order. - Sort neighbors by a stable key before pushing. For nodes, you need a key: maybe node.val, or an assigned unique index. If val is not unique, sorting by val alone isn’t stable. Better: assign each node a unique integer id at creation time. Example: class Node: _next_id = 0 def __init__(...): self.uid = Node._next_id Node._next_id += 1 Then sort neighbors by uid: nbrs = sorted(neighbors(node), key=lambda n: n.uid) for nxt in reversed(nbrs): stack.append(nxt) This gives deterministic order as long as uid assignment is deterministic (it will be if construction order is deterministic). Alternatively, if you already have stable source positions (like token index), use that.
Good idea. Another issue: sometimes I still see RecursionError but not from my traversal; it’s from __repr__ when printing nodes for debugging. It prints children recursively. How can I avoid that?

That’s common: dataclass auto-generated __repr__ will recursively print fields, causing deep recursion or cycles (parent pointers) to blow up. Fixes: 1) Disable dataclass repr for recursive fields: from dataclasses import dataclass, field @dataclass class Node: val: int left: "Node" = field(default=None, repr=False) right: "Node" = field(default=None, repr=False) parent: "Node" = field(default=None, repr=False) Then __repr__ prints only val. 2) Implement a custom __repr__ that prints val and maybe child vals/ids without recursing: def __repr__(self): return f"Node(val={self.val}, uid={self.uid})" 3) Use safe pretty-printers that detect cycles (some libraries do). Given parent pointers, I strongly recommend repr=False for parent at least.
That’s helpful. I’ll set repr=False for parent and maybe for children too. Now, about recursion limits: some colleagues suggested sys.setrecursionlimit(100000). Is that safe?

It can be dangerous. Python recursion uses the C call stack; setting recursionlimit too high can crash the interpreter with a segfault instead of a clean RecursionError. Also, deep recursion is slower than iterative loops. If you control input and can guarantee depth won’t exceed a safe amount, you can raise it moderately (e.g., a few thousand). But for “thousands deep” and potentially more, iterative is the robust fix. So: prefer iterative traversal or rewrite recursive algorithms into loops.

Makes sense. But I also have a recursive function that evaluates the expression tree, and rewriting it iteratively is more complex. Is there a pattern for evaluating deep expression trees without recursion?

Yes: use an explicit stack for postorder evaluation (like interpreting an AST). Two common strategies: 1) Postorder traversal with a visited flag: - Push (node, False). - Pop; if not processed, push (node, True) then push children (child, False). - When processed=True, compute node value from children results stored in a dict or stack. 2) Convert to Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) via shunting-yard or postorder, then evaluate iteratively. For a binary expression tree: def eval_expr(root): stack=[(root, False)] values={} while stack: node, done = stack.pop() if node is None: continue if done: if node.kind == "num": values[id(node)] = node.value else: a = values[id(node.left)] b = values[id(node.right)] values[id(node)] = apply(node.op, a, b) else: stack.append((node, True)) stack.append((node.right, False)) stack.append((node.left, False)) return values[id(root)] This handles depth without recursion. You can avoid dict by pushing results onto a value stack if the tree is strict and you can evaluate when you return, but dict-by-node is simplest. What does your Node look like for expressions (operators, literals)?
It’s like:

@dataclass(eq=False) class Expr: kind: str  # "num" or "bin" value: int = None op: str = None left: "Expr" = None right: "Expr" = None parent: "Expr" = field(default=None, repr=False) I want to evaluate it. Recursion breaks on deep nesting.

Use the postorder iterative approach. Here’s a concrete version: def apply(op, a, b): if op == "+": return a + b if op == "-": return a - b if op == "*": return a * b if op == "/": return a // b  # or true division raise ValueError(op) def eval_expr(root: Expr) -> int: values = {} stack = [(root, 0)]  # 0=first time, 1=children pushed while stack: node, state = stack.pop() if node is None: continue if state == 0: stack.append((node, 1)) if node.kind == "bin": stack.append((node.right, 0)) stack.append((node.left, 0)) else: if node.kind == "num": values[id(node)] = node.value else: a = values[id(node.left)] b = values[id(node.right)] values[id(node)] = apply(node.op, a, b) return values[id(root)] This will work for very deep trees. If memory is a concern, we can do it without storing values for all nodes by using a value stack and careful ordering, but this is usually fine.
I tried something similar but got KeyError for id(node.left) sometimes. I think some nodes have only one child (unary minus). How do I handle that?

Represent unary nodes explicitly (kind="unary") or allow bin with one child None. Then evaluation must handle missing children. Option 1: add kind="unary" with field child: But with your structure, simplest is to support kind "unary" using left as the operand and right=None. Then: if node.kind == "unary": a = values[id(node.left)] values[id(node)] = -a if node.op == "-" else ... Also in traversal, push left for unary. Update: if state == 0: stack.append((node, 1)) if node.kind == "bin": stack.append((node.right, 0)) stack.append((node.left, 0)) elif node.kind == "unary": stack.append((node.left, 0)) And in compute state: if node.kind == "num": ... elif node.kind == "unary": a = values[id(node.left)] values[id(node)] = -a if node.op == "-" else ... elif node.kind == "bin": a = values[id(node.left)] b = values[id(node.right)] ... If you currently encode unary by setting right=None but still kind="bin", you’ll get KeyError. Better to encode properly.
That clarifies it. Another debugging issue: I sometimes see RecursionError in equality checks or when adding nodes to sets/maps. Is that due to dataclass eq generating deep comparisons?

Yes. Dataclass-generated __eq__ compares field-by-field, which for recursive structures triggers recursive equality checks and can loop on cycles (parent pointers) or go very deep. That can cause RecursionError or extreme slowness. You already set eq=False in your Expr, which avoids generated __eq__. That’s good. If you need equality, define it in a non-recursive way, e.g., compare a unique uid or source span rather than full structure. Similarly, avoid using nodes as dict keys unless hashing is identity-based and eq is identity-based.
I see. I’ll keep eq=False. Switching topics slightly: how do I detect cycles robustly in my “tree” to ensure it’s actually a tree? I want an assertion check.

For a supposed tree with parent pointers: - Ensure exactly one root (parent=None). - Ensure each node’s children’s parent pointers point back. - Ensure no cycles. You can perform a DFS from root over children only, tracking visited. If you encounter a visited node again, you have a cycle or shared child. Example: def validate_tree(root): visited=set() stack=[root] while stack: node=stack.pop() if node is None: continue nid=id(node) if nid in visited: raise ValueError("Cycle or shared reference detected") visited.add(nid) for child in (node.left, node.right): if child is not None: if child.parent is not node: raise ValueError("Bad parent pointer") stack.append(child) return True If you also have parent pointers, you can verify by walking all nodes and ensuring root.parent is None and no other node has parent None (if it’s a single connected tree). But to check “exactly one root” you need all nodes; easiest is to collect nodes during traversal and then count parent None among them (should be 1 and should be root).

Great. My next question: I have a function that simplifies the expression tree recursively (constant folding). It also hits recursion depth. Is there a general approach to do tree rewrites iteratively?

Tree rewriting iteratively is trickier but doable with postorder traversal: compute simplified children first, then rebuild current node based on simplified children. That’s similar to evaluation, except you produce new nodes (or mutate in place). Pattern: - Postorder traverse nodes using stack with state. - Maintain a mapping from old node id to simplified node. - When processing a node after children done, create simplified version. Example skeleton: def simplify(root): simplified={} stack=[(root,0)] while stack: node,state=stack.pop() if node is None: continue if state==0: stack.append((node,1)) if node.kind in ("bin","unary"): if node.right: stack.append((node.right,0)) if node.left: stack.append((node.left,0)) else: if node.kind=="num": simplified[id(node)] = node elif node.kind=="unary": child = simplified[id(node.left)] # fold if child is num, etc. ... else: # bin L = simplified[id(node.left)] R = simplified[id(node.right)] # constant fold if L.kind=="num" and R.kind=="num": val = apply(node.op, L.value, R.value) simplified[id(node)] = Expr(kind="num", value=val) else: simplified[id(node)] = Expr(kind="bin", op=node.op, left=L, right=R) return simplified[id(root)] If you need parent pointers, you can set them afterward in a separate pass, or set them when constructing.
When I rebuild nodes, I lose sharing and parent links. I can rebuild parent links later, but I don’t want to allocate too many objects. Can I simplify in place iteratively?

Yes: in the postorder “compute” step, you can mutate node fields based on children. For constant folding: - If both children are numbers, turn current node into a number node: set kind="num", value=..., op=None, left=None, right=None. - For unary folding similarly. This keeps identity and parent pointers intact. Pseudo: if node.kind=="bin": if node.left.kind=="num" and node.right.kind=="num": node.kind="num" node.value=apply(...) node.op=None node.left=None node.right=None In iterative postorder, when you reach node in state==1, children are already simplified. Caveat: if other parts of the graph reference children nodes, mutating could be surprising. But you said it’s a tree (no sharing), so it’s fine.
That helps. Another pain point: sometimes I think I have a tree, but actually I have accidental cycles because I reuse nodes. How can I catch that during construction?

Add checks in your connect function: - Ensure child.parent is None before assigning (or equals parent if reattaching). - Ensure you don’t set a node as its own descendant. A simple immediate check: prevent self-linking: if left is parent or right is parent: raise if left is not None and left is right: raise To prevent descendant cycles, you’d need to check that parent is not in child’s ancestor chain before attaching: def attach(parent, child, side): # ensure child is not an ancestor of parent n=parent while n is not None: if n is child: raise ValueError("Cycle: attaching ancestor as child") n=n.parent # now attach if side=="left": parent.left=child else: parent.right=child if child: child.parent=parent That’s O(height) per attach, which might be fine.

Nice. Switching back: I still want to understand why my original recursive walk blew up even with visited. Could it be because I used recursion and the graph had many nodes, so recursion depth still grew?

Yes. Even without cycles, DFS recursion depth equals the maximum path length in the traversal tree (spanning tree). In a graph, DFS can still go very deep if there’s a long chain. visited prevents revisits, but doesn’t reduce depth. So recursion can still overflow. Iterative avoids that entirely.
Understood. Now I’m writing unit tests. Can you propose a minimal example that triggers infinite recursion with parent traversal and no visited?

Sure. Two nodes is enough: a = Node(1) b = Node(2) connect(a, left=b)  # b.parent = a walk(b, out)  # with parent recursion walk(b): - visit b - parent -> a walk(a): - visit a - left -> b walk(b) again … infinite. Even a single parent-child link creates a 2-cycle when you traverse both directions.
Perfect. I’ll include that in a test. Another question: if I do want recursion for readability, could I implement a trampoline or something in Python?

You can simulate recursion with a trampoline, but it ends up being an explicit stack anyway. Python does not optimize tail calls, so tail recursion doesn’t help. A trampoline approach would represent “next call” as a thunk and loop, but for tree recursion with multiple calls, it gets complex. So: explicit stack is the pragmatic approach.
Let’s talk about performance. My tree can have millions of nodes. Using id(node) and a visited set might be memory-heavy. Any alternatives?

If it’s a true tree and you only traverse children, you don’t need visited, so memory is O(height) for the stack only. If it’s a general graph and you must avoid revisits, visited is necessary unless you can mark nodes with a “color” field (visited flag) stored on the node itself. That can reduce overhead because you avoid a separate set, but you still store one bit per node (as an attribute). It may be cheaper than a Python set. Example: class Node: ... _mark = 0 global_mark increments each traversal: VISIT_TOKEN = object()  # or int generation But for speed: - Add node._seen_gen integer. - Maintain a global traversal generation counter. Pseudo: gen += 1 stack=[start] while stack: n=stack.pop() if n._seen_gen == gen: continue n._seen_gen = gen ... This avoids set allocations and hashing. It’s very effective for large graphs, but it mutates nodes and requires a field. If you can’t mutate, consider using a bitset keyed by uid (dense ids). If you have uid from 0..N-1, visited can be a bytearray or list of bools, far smaller than a set.
I do have sequential indices. I can assign uid during node creation. So a bytearray visited could work.

Yes. If uid is dense: visited = bytearray(n_nodes)  # 0/1 ... if visited[node.uid]: continue visited[node.uid]=1 That’s extremely memory efficient. If you don’t know n_nodes upfront, you can use a list and grow it, or use a dict/set until you finalize.
Good. One more bug: I used a default argument visited=set() in my recursive walk once, and it caused weird behavior across calls. Can you explain?

Mutable default arguments are evaluated once at function definition time, so the same set is reused across calls. That means nodes visited in a previous call remain marked, causing later traversals to skip nodes unexpectedly. Bad: def walk(node, out, visited=set()): ... Good: def walk(node, out, visited=None): if visited is None: visited=set()
Yep, that was it earlier. Thanks. Now, I’m also seeing RecursionError from a function that computes height:
def height(node): if not node: return 0 return 1 + max(height(node.left), height(node.right)) On deep chains it fails. How to compute height iteratively?

For a tree, compute height with iterative postorder and store heights. Option 1: use stack with state and a dict: def height(root): if root is None: return 0 h={} stack=[(root,0)] while stack: node,state=stack.pop() if node is None: continue if state==0: stack.append((node,1)) stack.append((node.right,0)) stack.append((node.left,0)) else: hl = h.get(id(node.left), 0) if node.left else 0 hr = h.get(id(node.right), 0) if node.right else 0 h[id(node)] = 1 + (hl if hl>hr else hr) return h[id(root)] If you have uid, use a list for heights. Option 2: BFS level order computes height as max depth if you traverse by levels, but that can be memory-heavy for wide trees. Postorder is usually better.
Great. Now: I also have a function that finds a node by value recursively. On deep trees, it fails. Iterative DFS should fix it, right?

Yes: def find(root, target): stack=[root] while stack: n=stack.pop() if n is None: continue if n.val == target: return n stack.append(n.right) stack.append(n.left) return None If graph with cycles, add visited.
I think I have enough to refactor. But I want to be sure about the original stack overflow: is it always RecursionError, or can Python crash?

Typically you’ll get RecursionError when Python detects recursion depth exceeded. But if you set recursionlimit too high, you can hit C stack overflow and crash (segfault) before Python can raise. Also, some recursion can happen in C extensions (like repr) that doesn’t check recursion depth the same way, sometimes leading to crashes. So rely on iterative for safety.
Understood. Could you help me craft a comprehensive debugging checklist for recursion issues in Python, specifically for tree/graph code?

Checklist: 1) Confirm base case: does it trigger for all paths? For recursion on nodes: if node is None return; if leaf return; etc. 2) Confirm progress: each call must move toward base case (e.g., child nodes, smaller problem). 3) Detect cycles: if structure can have back-edges (parent pointers, references), use visited. 4) Beware of implicit recursion: __repr__, __eq__, properties, iterators that traverse recursively. 5) Depth: compute/estimate max depth; if can exceed ~1000, use iterative or redesign. 6) Mutable default args: avoid visited=set() defaults. 7) Debug with instrumentation: - Print id(node), node.uid, depth counter. - Keep a recursion depth counter and assert it doesn’t exceed threshold. 8) Use sys.getrecursionlimit() only as a diagnostic, not a fix. 9) For graphs: ensure neighbor expansion is bounded and deterministic if needed; avoid iterating over sets without ordering in tests. 10) Add validation functions to detect cycles and bad parent pointers early.
That’s good. I’ll implement validation. I have another tricky case: I wrote a recursive generator that yields nodes:
def iter_nodes(node): if node is None: return yield node yield from iter_nodes(node.left) yield from iter_nodes(node.right) This also fails for deep trees. Is there an iterative generator pattern?

Yes, implement generator with an explicit stack: def iter_nodes(root): stack=[root] while stack: node=stack.pop() if node is None: continue yield node stack.append(node.right) stack.append(node.left) This yields preorder. For inorder/postorder you can use state flags. If graph, add visited.
For inorder, can you provide an iterative generator?

Inorder for binary tree: def inorder(root): stack=[] node=root while stack or node: while node: stack.append(node) node=node.left node=stack.pop() yield node node=node.right Works for deep trees without recursion.
Nice. For postorder?

Postorder iterative with two stacks or one stack with last-visited: Two-stack: def postorder(root): if root is None: return s1=[root] s2=[] while s1: n=s1.pop() s2.append(n) if n.left: s1.append(n.left) if n.right: s1.append(n.right) while s2: yield s2.pop() One-stack: def postorder(root): stack=[] last=None node=root while stack or node: if node: stack.append(node) node=node.left else: peek=stack[-1] if peek.right and last is not peek.right: node=peek.right else: yield peek last=stack.pop()
Great. I’ll use those. I think this covers it. Thanks for walking through everything.
Glad. If you paste one of the failing deep examples (even a synthetic chain builder), I can help verify the iterative versions and ensure they match the ordering you expect.
Here’s a synthetic deep chain that reproduces the recursion problem:

def make_chain(n): root = Expr(kind="num", value=0) cur = root for i in range(1, n): nxt = Expr(kind="num", value=i, parent=cur) cur.left = nxt cur = nxt return root, cur  # root and deepest leaf When I do list(iter_nodes(root)) with the recursive generator, it dies around n=2000.
