For a left-only chain, postorder should yield nodes from the deepest leaf back up to the root. If your simplify step expects children already simplified, you must process in postorder (children before parent). If you accidentally used preorder (parent first), the parent won’t see folded children yet. A quick sanity check: build a chain of unary minus nodes or binary nodes that can fold, and verify order. For postorder traversal of your left-only chain, the one-stack “last visited” version should work, but it’s easy to get wrong if the tree is mutated during traversal. Two reliable approaches for a postorder rewrite pass: 1) Use the “(node, state)” stack (most robust, mutation-friendly if you’re careful). 2) Use a two-stack postorder (also robust, but stores all nodes in s2, which is O(n) extra). Given you’re simplifying in place (mutating nodes), I recommend the (node, state) approach because it doesn’t rely on revisiting pointers the same way after mutation. Here’s a postorder iterator using state: def postorder_nodes(root): stack = [(root, 0)] while stack: node, state = stack.pop() if node is None: continue if state == 0: stack.append((node, 1)) # push children stack.append((node.right, 0)) stack.append((node.left, 0)) else: yield node If you run this on a left-only chain, you’ll get deepest-left first. Then simplify: def simplify_in_place(root): for node in postorder_nodes(root): if node.kind == "bin": L, R = node.left, node.right if L and R and L.kind == "num" and R.kind == "num": node.kind = "num" node.value = apply(node.op, L.value, R.value) node.op = None node.left = None node.right = None elif node.kind == "unary": c = node.left if c and c.kind == "num" and node.op == "-": node.kind = "num" node.value = -c.value node.op = None node.left = None node.right = None return root If your simplify “sometimes doesn’t simplify anything” on a chain, likely causes: - The traversal is actually preorder/inorder, not postorder. - Your folding condition never matches (e.g., nodes are kind="bin" but right is None; or kind labels differ). - You’re rebuilding nodes (creating new ones) but still returning/inspecting the old root. - You mutate node.kind/value but forget to clear op/children, so later checks don’t see kind=="num" as expected. If you paste your simplify function and what kinds/ops you expect in the chain (unary vs bin), I can pinpoint which condition isn’t firing.
Here’s my simplify function. It tries to fold “bin” nodes and also remove “+ 0” and “* 1” identities:

def simplify_in_place(root): stack = [(root, False)] while stack: node, done = stack.pop() if node is None: continue if not done: stack.append((node, True)) stack.append((node.right, False)) stack.append((node.left, False)) continue if node.kind == "bin": L = node.left R = node.right if L.kind == "num" and R.kind == "num": node.kind = "num" node.value = apply(node.op, L.value, R.value) node.op = None # I forgot to clear children maybe? elif node.op == "+" and R.kind == "num" and R.value == 0: # replace node with left node = L elif node.op == "*" and R.kind == "num" and R.value == 1: node = L return root This doesn’t seem to change the tree at all except maybe constant folding at the bottom.

Two main issues: 1) Rebinding the local name node (node = L) does not modify the actual tree. You need to update the parent’s pointer (or mutate the current node in place to become L). In your loop, node is just a local variable; assigning it won’t change whatever points to the original node. 2) When you fold to a num, you should clear left/right (and maybe parent links) or later logic may still treat it as having children. For in-place replacement without knowing the parent pointer in the traversal, you can mutate the current node to copy the child’s contents (a “node overwrite”). For example, to replace a bin node with its left child L: def overwrite(dst, src): dst.kind = src.kind dst.value = src.value dst.op = src.op dst.left = src.left dst.right = src.right # parent pointer should stay as-is on dst # but children’s parent pointers must be updated to dst if dst.left: dst.left.parent = dst if dst.right: dst.right.parent = dst Then in simplify: elif node.op == "+" and R and R.kind=="num" and R.value==0: overwrite(node, L) Similarly for *1. Also handle the symmetrical cases (0 + L, 1 * L) if they can appear. And for constant fold: if L and R and L.kind=="num" and R.kind=="num": node.kind="num" node.value=apply(...) node.op=None node.left=None node.right=None With these changes, you’ll see simplification propagate upward. One more subtlety: your traversal pushes node.right and node.left even when node.kind != "bin". That’s fine if left/right exist only for bins/unary, but you might want to guard to avoid attribute errors or unnecessary pushes. If you want, paste your apply and a sample expression builder (like building ((x + 0) + 0) chain), and I can show an end-to-end test that confirms the tree changed.

I added overwrite and clearing children. It works now. But I’m worried about parent pointers correctness after overwrite. In some cases, the simplified node’s old children still have parent pointing to the old child, not the overwritten node. You updated immediate children, but what about deeper descendants?

You only need to update the parent pointer of the immediate children you attach to dst. Deeper descendants already point to their own parents, which haven’t changed. Parent pointers are local relationships: each node’s parent should be the node that directly references it as left/right. So after overwrite: - dst.left.parent must be dst (if dst.left exists) - dst.right.parent must be dst Everything below that remains consistent. The only time you’d need to update deeper descendants is if you “splice” in an entire subtree but also changed internal structure; overwrite doesn’t do that. However, one caveat: if src is actually dst.left (or dst.right), and you overwrite dst with src, you’ve effectively removed src from the tree but src object still exists. That’s fine, but you should be careful not to leave dangling parent pointers pointing to removed nodes if you keep external references to them. If you want to avoid confusing stale nodes, another approach is to always allocate new nodes when simplifying and then rebuild parent pointers at the end; but you said you want to avoid allocations. Given it’s a tree and you don’t keep references to internal nodes elsewhere, overwrite is fine.
I do sometimes keep references to nodes for error reporting (like pointing to a subexpression). If simplify overwrites nodes, those references might now point to different expressions, which is bad.

Right—overwrite changes the meaning of that node identity, so any external references become misleading. If node identity matters externally, don’t overwrite. Instead, you need structural replacement: update the parent’s pointer to point to the replacement node, leaving the old node intact (or at least not repurposed). That requires access to the parent and which side you’re on. Since your nodes already have parent pointers, you can do: def replace_in_parent(node, new): p = node.parent if p is None: return new  # caller must update root reference if p.left is node: p.left = new elif p.right is node: p.right = new else: raise RuntimeError("Parent doesn't reference child") new.parent = p return None Then, when simplifying, instead of overwrite(node, L), you call replace_in_parent(node, L) and ensure L is detached correctly. But: your iterative postorder traversal stack already contains references to nodes. If you replace a node with one of its children, the old node remains in the stack processing order potentially. That’s okay if you just skip it after replacement, but you must be careful about what you return as root. A clean pattern is: return the new root from simplify, and have every rewrite return “replacement for this node”. That is easiest with recursion, but you can still do it iteratively with a mapping old->new (persistent nodes), which does allocate new nodes or at least changes pointers. If you want no overwrites and minimal allocations, you can: - Use replace_in_parent for identity eliminations (+0, *1), which doesn’t allocate. - For constant folding, you can either overwrite (changes identity) or allocate a new num node and replace the old node in parent. If identity matters, allocate. So tradeoff: - Preserve identity => replacements via parent links, possibly allocate for folds. - Max speed/min allocations => overwrite, but identity changes. Which is more important in your project: stable node identity for diagnostics, or in-place performance?
Stable identity is important. I’ll allocate new nodes for folds and do replace_in_parent for identity eliminations. How do I do this safely in an iterative postorder pass without missing nodes?

Use a postorder stack of the original nodes, but when computing the simplified result for a node, produce a “current representative” node (which might be one of its children, or a new node), and store it in a dict keyed by id(original_node). Then parents use the simplified children from the dict, not the original pointers. That avoids needing to mutate pointers mid-traversal and avoids missing nodes. Sketch: def simplify(root): rep = {}  # id(old) -> new node stack=[(root,0)] while stack: node,state=stack.pop() if node is None: continue if state==0: stack.append((node,1)) stack.append((node.right,0)) stack.append((node.left,0)) else: if node.kind=="num": rep[id(node)] = node elif node.kind=="bin": L = rep[id(node.left)] if node.left else None R = rep[id(node.right)] if node.right else None # constant fold if L and R and L.kind=="num" and R.kind=="num": rep[id(node)] = Expr(kind="num", value=apply(node.op, L.value, R.value)) # +0, *1 eliminations elif node.op=="+" and R and R.kind=="num" and R.value==0: rep[id(node)] = L elif node.op=="+" and L and L.kind=="num" and L.value==0: rep[id(node)] = R elif node.op=="*" and R and R.kind=="num" and R.value==1: rep[id(node)] = L elif node.op=="*" and L and L.kind=="num" and L.value==1: rep[id(node)] = R else: # rebuild node (new) to keep identity stable? identity stable means keep old nodes too # But if you keep old identity, you'd be mutating. Since you want stability of existing nodes referenced elsewhere, # you should build a new node for any changed structure, and leave old node untouched. rep[id(node)] = Expr(kind="bin", op=node.op, left=L, right=R) # unary similar... new_root = rep[id(root)] # fix parent pointers in the new tree set_parents(new_root, None) return new_root This approach constructs a new simplified tree (persistent), leaving the old tree untouched, so any external references remain valid (they reference the old version). If you want to preserve identity for nodes that didn’t change, you can reuse them (rep points to original node) but then you must ensure their child pointers match the simplified children, which would mutate. So full persistence is simplest for correctness. If you don’t want to rebuild everything, you can do a hybrid: - If rep children are identical to original children and no rule applies, reuse original node. - Otherwise allocate a new node. But careful: if you reuse original node but its child should be replaced by a simplified child, you’d have to mutate it—breaking identity semantics. So “reuse original node” only when the entire subtree under it is unchanged and you’re okay with sharing old nodes in the new tree. That’s fine; it doesn’t mutate, it just points to old nodes. But then the new tree shares structure with the old, which might be okay. To avoid parent-pointer confusion with sharing, you may want to omit parent pointers entirely in the persistent version, or accept that a node can’t have two parents. If you share nodes between old and new trees, parent pointers become ambiguous. So if you keep parent pointers, don’t share nodes; rebuild all nodes in the new tree (or at least rebuild any node that would end up with a different parent). Given you have parent pointers, the safest is: always allocate new nodes for the simplified tree, then set parent pointers in that new tree. That’s memory-heavy but consistent.

Good point about parent pointers and sharing. I think I’ll drop parent pointers from the simplified tree and keep them only in the original parse tree. Then the simplified tree is immutable-ish. That should reduce bugs.

That’s a solid design: keep the parse tree with rich links for diagnostics, and a separate simplified/evaluation representation without parent pointers (or with them computed on demand). If you keep the simplified tree without parent pointers, you can safely share unchanged subtrees between versions, because there’s no “two parents” invariant to violate. Then your simplify mapping can reuse nodes when unchanged, and allocate only when needed.
Last thing: when I debug, I often do print(root) and it’s still huge. Any tips to debug deep trees without blowing up output?

A few practical tactics: 1) Add a compact __repr__ that shows only a small summary: - node kind/op/value and maybe uid. - Don’t print children recursively. 2) Write a “preview” function with depth/width limits: def preview(node, max_depth=5, max_nodes=50): out=[] stack=[(node,0)] seen=0 while stack and seen<max_nodes: n,d=stack.pop() if n is None: continue out.append("  "*d + f"{n.kind}:{n.op or n.value}") seen += 1 if d < max_depth: stack.append((n.right, d+1)) stack.append((n.left, d+1)) else: # indicate truncation if there are children if n.left or n.right: out.append("  "*(d+1) + "...") return "\n".join(out) 3) Log just the path to the problematic node (ancestors) rather than the whole tree. 4) For recursion debugging specifically: print depth counters and node ids, not full nodes.
That’s exactly what I needed. I’ll implement preview and drop recursive repr. Thanks again.
I’m designing a REST API for a personal project and want feedback on endpoint structure. Is it better to use /users/{id}/posts or /posts?user_id={id} for fetching a user's posts?

Either can be “right,” but they communicate slightly different meanings and have different tradeoffs. - /users/{id}/posts is a nested resource that reads as “posts belonging to this user.” It’s great when the parent-child relationship is central and you want a clear hierarchy. It also naturally scopes the collection, and you can add subresources like /users/{id}/posts/{postId}. - /posts?user_id={id} treats posts as the primary collection and “user_id” as a filter. It’s great when posts are queried in many different ways (by user, tag, date range, status, search) and you want one consistent entry point for listing posts with filters. A common pattern: expose /posts for general listing/search with filters, and optionally also provide /users/{id}/posts as a convenience alias that maps internally to the same query. If you pick only one, I’d lean toward /posts?user_id=... if you expect many filters and consistent listing semantics; otherwise /users/{id}/posts is clean and self-descriptive.
I do expect lots of filtering: tags, date ranges, and maybe full-text search later. But I also like the readability of nested endpoints. Is having both considered bad design?

It’s not inherently bad, but it introduces duplication, which can create inconsistencies if not managed carefully. If you offer both: - Make sure they behave identically in terms of pagination, sorting, filtering semantics, response shape, and error codes. - Document that /users/{id}/posts is equivalent to /posts?user_id={id}. - Consider canonical URLs: you can return a “self” link in each post that points to the canonical resource location (likely /posts/{postId}). For the collection, you can decide one canonical listing endpoint and treat the other as an alias. When duplication becomes problematic: - If different teams/clients start relying on subtle differences, you’ll get stuck supporting both “flavors.” - If you evolve filtering/pagination, you must update both. A pragmatic approach is: choose /posts as the canonical list endpoint, add /users/{id}/posts only if you have a strong usability reason. If you do add it, implement it as a thin wrapper around the same handler.
How do you decide what should be canonical? Like, should the “self” URL of a post always be /posts/{id} even if it’s under a user?

For a resource that has a stable identity independent of its parent, the canonical “self” is usually the shortest non-ambiguous one: /posts/{postId}. Reasons: - A post typically exists regardless of how you navigate to it (via user, via search, via tag). - If you use nested identifiers like /users/{userId}/posts/{postId}, you must validate that the post belongs to that user, and you’ve now got multiple possible URLs for the same resource. - Canonical URLs make caching, linking, and deduplication simpler. That said, nested “contextual” URLs can still be useful: - /users/{userId}/posts/{postId} can be treated as a contextual view or simply a convenience route that redirects (301/302) to /posts/{postId}, or returns the same representation after verifying relationship. So: canonical self link: /posts/{postId}. Collection links can be contextual: /users/{userId}/posts, /tags/{tagId}/posts, etc., but it’s often better to keep listing under /posts with filters.
That makes sense. What about creating a post? Would I POST to /posts with user_id in body, or POST to /users/{id}/posts?

Both are viable; the decision hinges on how you model ownership and authorization. Option A: POST /posts with user_id in the request body - Pros: one creation endpoint for posts; consistent with “posts are top-level.” - Cons: you must validate user_id is allowed/exists; clients could attempt to create posts under other users unless you ignore/override user_id based on auth context. Option B: POST /users/{id}/posts - Pros: ownership is explicit in the URL; often easier to reason about; you can enforce that the path userId matches the authenticated user or an allowed scope. - Cons: if you later allow posts to change ownership (rare) or have multiple “owners,” the nested endpoint becomes awkward. A common compromise: - If posts are always owned by the authenticated principal, don’t accept user_id at all. POST /posts with no user_id; server sets owner from auth token. Filtering by “my posts” can be /posts?mine=true or /users/me/posts. - If you must create on behalf of others (admin flows), you can keep POST /posts with user_id but require elevated permission. If you’re trying to keep it purely REST-ish and consistent: POST /posts is fine; treat user_id as immutable set at creation (or derived from auth), and expose /users/{id}/posts as a read-only convenience.
I like the idea of /users/me/posts. Is “me” a good practice? Or should it always be /users/{id}?

“me” is common and generally accepted as a convenience because it reduces client-side state and avoids needing to know the user’s numeric/UUID id. Typical patterns: - GET /users/me returns the current user profile. - GET /users/me/posts returns current user’s posts. - PATCH /users/me updates current user profile. Caveats: - Decide whether “me” is only for the authenticated user (usually yes). If unauthenticated, return 401. - Document it clearly; it’s a special identifier, not an actual user id. - Keep authorization consistent: /users/{id} should still exist for fetching other users if allowed; /users/me is just a shortcut. If you want to be strict, you can avoid it, but in many APIs it’s a net win for ergonomics.
Switching gears: how would you recommend modeling pagination? Offset/limit, page/pageSize, or cursor-based?

Cursor-based pagination is the most robust for mutable datasets, but offset/limit is simplest. Offset/limit (e.g., /posts?limit=20&offset=40) - Pros: easy to implement and understand; jump to arbitrary page. - Cons: can return duplicates or skip items if the underlying data changes; performance can degrade with large offsets depending on storage. Page/pageSize (e.g., page=3&pageSize=20) - Essentially offset under the hood; same pros/cons; sometimes friendlier for UI. Cursor-based (e.g., /posts?limit=20&cursor=eyJpZCI6... or /posts?limit=20&after=postId) - Pros: stable traversal; better performance when using indexed keyset pagination; avoids deep offset. - Cons: harder for clients to jump to arbitrary pages; more complex to implement; must define ordering. Recommendation: - If you expect growth and frequent new content, use cursor-based with a deterministic sort order (often created_at desc, id desc as tiebreaker). - Return pagination metadata via Link headers (RFC 5988 style) or in the response body: next_cursor, prev_cursor, has_more. - Keep the query parameters consistent across endpoints.
If I use cursor-based, what should the cursor encode? Just an ID? Or timestamp+ID?

Prefer a cursor that corresponds to your sort key(s). If you sort by created_at desc, you need enough data to resume precisely. Common patterns: 1) after_id / before_id with ordering by id - Works if id is monotonically increasing and correlates with creation time (often true for numeric sequences, not always for UUID). - If using UUIDv4, ordering by id is meaningless; don’t do this. 2) after=timestamp,id (keyset) - Sort by created_at desc, id desc as tiebreaker. - Cursor encodes the last seen (created_at, id). Next page query uses: WHERE (created_at < last_created_at) OR (created_at = last_created_at AND id < last_id) - This is stable and efficient with an index on (created_at, id). 3) opaque base64/json cursor - Encode the tuple (created_at, id) into an opaque string so clients treat it as a token. - Pros: you can change internal structure later; clients don’t rely on semantics. - Cons: debugging is a bit harder; but you can still provide a “debug” mode in logs. So: timestamp+ID is a solid choice unless your sorting is different (e.g., updated_at, score).
What about sorting and filtering conventions? Should I do /posts?sort=-created_at or /posts?order=desc&sort=created_at?

Both are common; the “-field” convention is compact and supports multi-sort naturally. Option: sort=-created_at,title - Here “-” means descending, no prefix means ascending. - Multi-sort: sort=-created_at,id - Easy to parse and expressive. Alternative: sort=created_at&order=desc - Simpler for single sort but awkward for multi-sort (you need arrays or repeated params). I’d recommend: - sort parameter that accepts comma-separated fields. - Use “-” prefix for descending. - Validate allowed fields; reject unknown sort fields with 400 and a helpful message (don’t silently ignore). Filtering: - Keep it predictable: /posts?tag=python&status=published - For ranges: created_at_gte=...&created_at_lt=... or created_at[from]=...&created_at[to]=... - Pick one style and apply consistently.
I’ve seen APIs that use filter[status]=published. Is that better?

That style is often associated with JSON:API and some conventions that group filters under filter[...] keys. It can be nice if you have many query params and want to avoid collisions with reserved params (sort, page, include). Pros of filter[status]: - Namespacing keeps query clean: filter[status], filter[tag], filter[created_at][gte], etc. - Easier to extend without conflicts. Cons: - Slightly more verbose; some tooling doesn’t handle bracket syntax as nicely (though most do). - If you’re not following JSON:API otherwise, it can feel inconsistent. If you want a simple custom API: - Use flat params for common filters. - Reserve a few keywords: sort, limit, cursor, include, fields. If you want a more structured standard: - Consider JSON:API style more broadly (including pagination and sparse fieldsets), but it’s a bigger commitment.
Speaking of include and fields: should I support embedding related resources? Like /posts?include=author,comments?

If clients benefit from reducing round trips, yes, but it adds complexity and can cause performance pitfalls. A common approach: - include=author,comments - Response includes related objects either embedded or in a side-loaded section (depending on your style). - Also support sparse fieldsets: fields[post]=id,title,created_at&fields[user]=id,name Tradeoffs: - You need to prevent “include explosion” (author.comments.author...). - You need to define max depth and allowed includes. - You must handle N+1 queries; implement efficient joins/batching. If you keep it simpler: - Provide basic embedding like include=author only, not deep graphs. - For comments, consider separate endpoints with pagination: /posts/{id}/comments. A pragmatic rule: - Embed small, commonly needed relations (author summary). - Keep large collections (comments, likes) separate.
For errors, what response format do you recommend? Just { "error": "message" }?

Minimal is fine, but you’ll want structure for programmatic handling and validation errors. A common, practical error shape: { "error": { "code": "validation_error", "message": "Invalid request", "details": [ { "field": "title", "issue": "required" }, { "field": "created_at", "issue": "invalid_format" } ], "request_id": "..." } } Key ideas: - code: stable machine-readable identifier (snake_case). - message: human-readable summary. - details: optional array for field-level issues. - request_id: helpful for tracing (even if you don’t expose internal logs, it’s good practice). Also align HTTP status codes: - 400 for invalid query/body. - 401 unauthenticated. - 403 unauthorized. - 404 not found. - 409 conflict (e.g., unique constraint). - 422 sometimes used for validation, but 400 is also acceptable; pick one and be consistent.
I’m considering versioning. Should I do /v1/posts or use Accept headers?

URL path versioning (/v1/...) is easiest to operate and debug. Path versioning: - Pros: visible, cache-friendly, simple routing, easy for clients. - Cons: can encourage “big bang” versions rather than incremental evolution. Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapi+json;version=1): - Pros: keeps URLs clean; more “REST-pure.” - Cons: harder to test in browser; some proxies/caches behave differently; more client complexity. Many public APIs choose /v1 for pragmatism. If you do: - Keep v1 stable; avoid breaking changes. - Use additive changes when possible. - Consider deprecation policy if you ever introduce v2.
What counts as a breaking change? Is adding a field breaking?

Generally: - Adding a new optional field to responses is non-breaking (clients should ignore unknown fields). - Removing a field is breaking. - Changing a field type/meaning is breaking. - Renaming fields is breaking. - Changing default sort order can be breaking (subtle but real for clients). - Changing pagination semantics (e.g., from offset to cursor) is breaking unless you support both. - Tightening validation (rejecting inputs previously accepted) can be breaking. For requests: - Adding a new required field is breaking. - Adding a new optional field is non-breaking. - Changing enum values can be breaking. So adding a response field is usually safe; still document it.
About HTTP methods: for updating posts, should I use PUT or PATCH?

PATCH is usually better for partial updates; PUT is for full replacement. PATCH /posts/{id} - Client sends only fields to change (e.g., { "title": "New" }). - Server updates those fields, leaving others unchanged. - Must define behavior for nulls (clear vs ignore). Usually null means set to null, unless you prohibit. PUT /posts/{id} - Client sends complete representation; omitted fields may be removed/reset. - Good when clients always have the full resource state. Most APIs: - Use PATCH for updates. - Optionally support PUT if you have a strong use case. If you implement PATCH, consider JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396) or JSON Patch (RFC 6902). Merge Patch is simpler.
If I go with JSON Merge Patch, do I need special content-type?

Ideally yes, to be explicit. - Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json for JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396). - For standard PATCH with a custom partial object, many APIs just use application/json, but then semantics are “whatever the API says.” If you want clarity and interoperability: - Use application/merge-patch+json for PATCH. - Document how nulls behave (Merge Patch treats null as setting the field to null; removing a field is done by setting it to null, which may differ from “delete property” semantics in some models). If you don’t want to deal with special content-type, you can still use application/json and define your own patch semantics, but it’s less standard.
For deleting a post, is it okay to do soft delete? How should the API represent that?

Soft delete is common. API-wise, you have a few options: 1) DELETE /posts/{id} performs soft delete - Resource becomes inaccessible in normal GET (404 or 410). - You may allow admins to retrieve it with include_deleted=true. - Pros: matches client expectation of “delete.” - Cons: semantics are slightly hidden. 2) PATCH /posts/{id} { "deleted": true } or { "status": "deleted" } - Explicit; allows undo. - But clients might still expect DELETE. 3) Separate endpoint: POST /posts/{id}/delete - Generally less RESTful; not needed. Common practice: - Use DELETE for the action. - Return 204 No Content on success. - Decide whether subsequent GET returns 404 (pretend it never existed) or 410 Gone (explicitly gone). 404 is more common. - Provide query filter: /posts?status=deleted (if you expose deleted items to privileged contexts).
Let’s talk about IDs. Should I use integer IDs or UUIDs in URLs?

Depends on your priorities. Integer IDs: - Pros: shorter URLs, easier to read, often faster indexing. - Cons: predictable; can enable enumeration (though authorization should prevent data leakage anyway). UUIDs (v4): - Pros: hard to guess; good for distributed ID generation. - Cons: longer; less index-friendly (random distribution) unless you use UUIDv7/ULID; cursor pagination by id doesn’t make sense. If you want “harder to guess” and better ordering: - Consider UUIDv7 or ULID: roughly time-ordered, still globally unique, more index-friendly than v4, and supports keyset pagination by id if you sort by id. Authorization still matters either way; don’t rely on unguessable IDs as security.
I might use ULIDs. If ULIDs are sortable, does that mean I can just paginate by id?

If you sort by ULID (ascending or descending), yes, you can do keyset pagination by id because ULIDs are time-ordered. Caveats: - ULID ordering corresponds to generation time, not necessarily “created_at” if records can be imported or created with custom timestamps. - If you store created_at and it can differ, decide which is the authoritative ordering. - ULIDs can collide in ordering if generated in the same millisecond, but they still remain unique; ordering is stable. Implementation: - Sort by id desc for “newest first.” - Cursor: last_id. - Query: WHERE id < last_id ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT n. If you need ordering by other fields (score, updated_at), you’ll still need composite cursors.
Good point. How about naming conventions: snake_case vs camelCase in JSON?

Pick one and be consistent. Most REST APIs either: - Use snake_case in JSON (common in many backend ecosystems). - Use camelCase in JSON (common in JS-heavy ecosystems). Considerations: - If your clients are primarily JavaScript, camelCase feels natural. - If your storage/models are snake_case, you might prefer snake_case to reduce mapping. Either is fine. If you choose snake_case: - created_at, user_id, is_published. If camelCase: - createdAt, userId, isPublished. Avoid mixing. Also be consistent in query params (match JSON naming if possible).
I’m leaning snake_case. For endpoints, should I use plural nouns always? /posts, /users?

Yes, plural nouns for collections is the most common convention: - /posts for the collection. - /posts/{id} for an item. For subresources: - /posts/{id}/comments (collection) - /posts/{id}/comments/{commentId} Avoid verbs in paths when possible. Use HTTP methods to express actions.
What about actions that don’t fit CRUD, like “publish” a post?

Two common approaches: 1) Model it as a state change: - PATCH /posts/{id} with { "status": "published" } or { "published_at": "..."}. This keeps it resource-oriented. 2) Action endpoint: - POST /posts/{id}/publish - POST /posts/{id}/unpublish This is explicit and can be easier if publishing triggers workflows. If publish is just a field change, PATCH is cleaner. If publish triggers side effects (notifications, indexing, etc.), action endpoints can be acceptable. You can still keep it resource-ish by treating “publish” as creating a “publication” subresource: - POST /posts/{id}/publications - DELETE /posts/{id}/publications/{pubId} But that can be overkill.
For concurrency control, should I use ETags?

If you expect concurrent edits, ETags with If-Match are a solid solution. Flow: - GET /posts/{id} returns ETag: "abc123" (often a hash or version number). - Client PATCH /posts/{id} with header If-Match: "abc123". - If the resource changed, server returns 412 Precondition Failed. Implementation detail: - Use a version column (integer increment) or updated_at timestamp to generate ETag. - Ensure ETag changes whenever representation changes. If you don’t implement ETags, you can still do optimistic locking with a version field in the body, but headers are more standard.
Should ETag represent the full response or just the resource version?

For concurrency control, it’s best if it corresponds to the resource state you’re protecting—typically a version number or updated_at. It doesn’t need to be a hash of the full body as long as it changes when the resource changes. Strong vs weak ETags: - Strong ETag means byte-for-byte representation match. - Weak ETag (prefixed with W/) means semantically equivalent. For optimistic locking: - A strong ETag based on version is fine. For caching: - Strong ETag based on representation also works but may be more expensive. Many APIs use a simple strong ETag derived from version: ETag: "v17".
What about caching for list endpoints? Is it worth adding Cache-Control?

It can be, but list endpoints are tricky because they change often and have many query combinations. For item endpoints: - GET /posts/{id} can have Cache-Control: private, max-age=0 and use ETag for revalidation. - Or if public data: Cache-Control: public, max-age=60 with ETag. For list endpoints: - If content changes frequently, you might still allow short caching: max-age=10-30. - If personalized (depends on auth): use private caching or no-store. - Vary headers matter: Vary: Authorization, Accept, etc. If you don’t want complexity, start with ETag on item endpoints and minimal caching on lists, then optimize later.
On authentication: I’m thinking Bearer tokens. Should I put them in headers only?

Yes, Authorization: Bearer <token> in headers is standard. Avoid putting tokens in query params: - They can leak via logs, referrers, browser history. - Harder to protect. Also consider: - Return 401 when missing/invalid. - Return 403 when valid but insufficient permission. - Provide WWW-Authenticate header on 401 if you want to be formal.
For rate limiting, how do I expose limits to clients?

Common response headers: - X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000 - X-RateLimit-Remaining: 742 - X-RateLimit-Reset: unix_timestamp There’s also a more standardized approach emerging (RateLimit headers, RFC 9239): - RateLimit-Limit: 1000;w=3600 - RateLimit-Remaining: 742 - RateLimit-Reset: 1234 On limit exceeded: - 429 Too Many Requests - Include Retry-After header (seconds or date). Pick one approach and document it. If you don’t need it yet, you can design for it without implementing immediately.
Should 429 responses include a JSON body?
It’s helpful. For example: { "error": { "code": "rate_limited", "message": "Too many requests", "retry_after": 30 } } Still include Retry-After header as the primary machine-readable hint.
I’m also thinking about idempotency for POST requests, like creating posts. Should I support Idempotency-Key?

If clients might retry POST due to network timeouts, Idempotency-Key is valuable. Pattern: - Client sends POST /posts with header Idempotency-Key: <unique key>. - Server stores the key with the result for some TTL. - If the same key is reused with the same endpoint and same authenticated identity, return the original response instead of creating a duplicate. Considerations: - Scope idempotency to (auth identity, endpoint). - Decide TTL (e.g., 24 hours). - Handle mismatched payload with same key: return 409 or 422 with a clear error. This is especially useful for payment-like operations; for posts it’s still nice if duplication would be annoying.
For response codes on creation: 201 with Location header?
Yes: - Return 201 Created. - Include Location: /posts/{newId}. - Return the created resource in the body (common) or just minimal info (id, links). Returning the full resource is usually convenient.
How should I handle bulk operations? Like creating multiple posts at once.

Bulk endpoints are often non-standard but practical. Options: 1) POST /posts/bulk - Body: { "items": [ ... ] } - Response: per-item status results. 2) POST /posts with array body - Body: [ {post1}, {post2} ] - Less explicit; can conflict with single-create semantics. I prefer explicit /posts/bulk or /bulk/posts. Design points: - Decide all-or-nothing vs partial success. - Partial success: return 207 Multi-Status (WebDAV) or 200 with per-item results. Many APIs avoid 207 and use 200. - Include per-item error details with index or client-provided correlation id. Example response: { "results": [ { "client_id": "a1", "status": 201, "id": "..." }, { "client_id": "a2", "status": 400, "error": {...} } ] }
Should bulk delete be a DELETE with a body? I’ve heard bodies on DELETE are controversial.

Right: DELETE with a body is technically allowed by HTTP but not consistently supported by clients/proxies. Safer patterns: - POST /posts/bulk-delete with body containing ids - Or POST /posts/bulk with an action field (less clean) - Or DELETE /posts?ids=1,2,3 (but URL length limits) So for bulk delete, POST /posts/bulk-delete is pragmatic.
For searching posts, should I do /posts/search?q=... or just /posts?q=...?

Prefer /posts?q=... (or /posts?query=...) if search is just another filter on the collection. It keeps one entry point for listing. Use /posts/search if: - Search behaves very differently than listing (different ranking, different response shape). - You want to clearly separate “search index” behavior from database listing. If you plan to add full-text ranking, highlights, facets: - /posts/search can be reasonable. But you can still keep /posts with q and add params like sort=relevance when q is present.
Facets might be useful later. Would that push toward /posts/search?

It might. Faceted search often returns extra metadata (facets, counts) beyond a normal list. Two approaches: - Keep /posts as canonical list, and when q is present allow facets=true to include facet info. Response becomes more complex but still manageable. - Create /posts/search that returns: { "items": [...], "facets": {...}, "total": ... } while /posts remains simpler. If you expect two distinct use cases (simple listing vs rich search), separating endpoints can keep each response stable and easier to consume.
For total counts, should list endpoints return total number of items?

Total counts can be expensive on large datasets, especially with complex filters. Options: - Always return total: convenient for UIs but potentially costly. - Return total optionally: /posts?include_total=true. Default false. - Return an approximate count (if your storage supports it). - Return “has_more” with cursor pagination and omit total. If you’re using cursor-based pagination, total is less natural. Many cursor APIs omit total and provide has_more/next_cursor. If you need UI page counts, you can add an endpoint like /posts/count?filters... or include_total=true.
How should I represent timestamps? ISO 8601 strings?

Yes. Use ISO 8601 with timezone (UTC recommended), e.g.: - "2026-04-02T13:45:30Z" Avoid ambiguous formats. Also be consistent about precision (seconds vs milliseconds). If you include milliseconds, do it everywhere: "2026-04-02T13:45:30.123Z".
For boolean query params like mine=true, do you recommend true/false or 1/0?

true/false is clearer. Accepting both can be user-friendly, but be careful about ambiguity. If you accept both: - Normalize "true", "false", "1", "0". - Reject other values with 400. Document canonical values as true/false.
How should I handle unknown query params? Ignore or error?

For public APIs, I prefer erroring on unknown query params (400) because: - It catches typos early (srot instead of sort). - It prevents clients from thinking a filter is applied when it isn’t. But there’s a tradeoff: - Strictness can break clients if you later introduce new params and old servers reject them (if clients talk to multiple versions). If you version in path, that’s less of a concern. A middle ground: - Strict in v1: unknown params error. - Or allow unknown but include a response warning header (rarely implemented). I’d choose strict for a cleaner developer experience.
For response envelopes, should I wrap everything in { data: ... }?

Wrapping is optional. Pros of envelope: - You can include metadata consistently: { "data": [...], "meta": {...}, "links": {...} }. - Easier to extend without changing top-level type. Cons: - Adds verbosity. - Some clients prefer direct arrays/objects. A common compromise: - For list endpoints: envelope with data + pagination metadata. - For single resource: either return the object directly or still wrap in data for consistency. If you want consistency and future-proofing, envelope everything. If you want simplicity, don’t envelope single resources, but be prepared to add headers for metadata.
If I envelope lists, what fields do you suggest?

Example: { "data": [ ...posts... ], "meta": { "limit": 20, "next_cursor": "abc", "has_more": true }, "links": { "self": "/posts?limit=20", "next": "/posts?limit=20&cursor=abc" } } This gives clients both machine-friendly cursor and navigable links. If you use offset pagination, meta could include offset and total (optional).
How do you feel about HATEOAS? Should I include links everywhere?

Full HATEOAS (discoverability-driven clients) is rarely implemented end-to-end outside specific ecosystems. But including some links can still be helpful. Practical approach: - Include a self link for resources if you have consistent canonical URLs. - Include pagination links for lists (self/next/prev). - Optionally include related links (author, comments) if it helps. Don’t feel obligated to make the entire API navigable solely through links. Most clients still use documentation.
For nested resources like comments, would you do /posts/{id}/comments and /comments/{id} both?

Similar to posts/users discussion: - /comments/{commentId} as canonical item endpoint. - /posts/{postId}/comments as collection endpoint. You can optionally also allow /comments?post_id=... for filtering across contexts. But if comments are primarily accessed per post, /posts/{id}/comments is very natural. If comments can be searched globally, then /comments with filters makes sense.
If I do /posts/{id}/comments, should creating a comment be POST there?

Yes, that’s a good use of nesting because the comment’s parent is essential at creation: - POST /posts/{postId}/comments with body { "body": "...", ... } Server sets post_id from the path. The created comment can still have canonical URL /comments/{commentId} in Location header.
How should I handle validation errors for nested routes? Like posting a comment to a non-existent post.

If the parent doesn’t exist: - Return 404 Not Found for /posts/{postId}. Even though the request is “create comment,” the path implies the post must exist. If the post exists but you can’t comment due to permissions/state: - 403 Forbidden (no permission) or 409 Conflict (post locked/archived) depending on semantics. If the body is invalid: - 400 (or 422 if you choose that) with details about fields.
For media uploads, would you design a separate upload endpoint or support multipart on /posts?

Depends on your needs. Option A: Direct multipart upload to /posts - POST /posts with Content-Type: multipart/form-data including fields + file. - Pros: simple for clients; one step. - Cons: harder to scale; mixes concerns; may complicate validation and retries. Option B: Separate uploads resource - POST /uploads to get an upload id or a signed URL. - Then POST /posts with attachment references (upload_id or URL). - Pros: more scalable; resumable; allows virus scanning; decouples storage. - Cons: more steps. If you expect small attachments and simplicity matters, multipart on /posts is fine. If you expect larger files or want robust upload handling, use separate upload flow.
If I do separate uploads, how should the upload resource look?

One pattern: 1) POST /uploads Request: { "filename": "image.png", "content_type": "image/png", "size": 12345 } Response: { "data": { "id": "upl_...", "upload_url": "https://storage...signed...", "expires_at": "..." } } 2) Client PUTs file bytes to upload_url. 3) Client creates post: POST /posts { "title": "...", "attachment_ids": ["upl_..."] } Optionally: - GET /uploads/{id} to check status (pending, complete, failed). - Server finalizes and makes an attachment resource: /attachments/{id}.
Back to endpoint naming: do you prefer kebab-case or snake_case in paths?

Most APIs use lowercase with hyphens for readability in URLs (kebab-case), but snake_case is also seen. Examples: - /user-profiles vs /user_profiles Pick one. I tend to prefer kebab-case in paths because underscores can be less visible in some contexts, and hyphens are more common in URLs. But if you’re using snake_case everywhere else, snake_case in paths is fine. Consistency matters more than the specific choice.
If I use kebab-case in paths but snake_case in JSON, is that weird?

It’s not uncommon. URLs and JSON are different surfaces. As long as each is consistent internally, it’s fine: - Paths: /user-profiles, /posts - JSON: created_at, user_id Just document conventions clearly.
How should I handle partial responses? Like selecting fields to reduce payload.

Sparse fieldsets are useful: - /posts?fields=id,title,created_at Or namespaced: - /posts?fields[post]=id,title,created_at If you also allow include=author: - fields[user]=id,name Be careful: - Ensure required fields for links/ids are always present, or define rules. - Validate unknown fields with 400. - Consider default field sets so clients don’t accidentally omit something important.
Should I allow clients to request expansions via “expand” instead of “include”?

“include” and “expand” are essentially the same idea. Pick one term and stick to it. “include” is common in JSON:API-like designs; “expand” is common in some other APIs. If “include” is already used for something else, “expand” can be clearer.
What about HTTP status for empty list? 200 with empty array?
Yes: - 200 OK with data: [] (or [] if not enveloped). Don’t use 204 for empty lists; it complicates clients and loses metadata like pagination.
For 404 responses, should I return a body?
Usually yes, with your standard error format. Some clients rely on it, and it helps debugging. Example: Status: 404 { "error": { "code": "not_found", "message": "Post not found" } }
How do you recommend handling content negotiation? Just JSON?

If you only support JSON, keep it simple: - Accept: application/json - Content-Type: application/json You can still be strict: - If Content-Type is not application/json on POST/PATCH, return 415 Unsupported Media Type. - If Accept requests something you don’t support, return 406 Not Acceptable (many APIs ignore Accept and always return JSON, but strictness is fine). If you plan to support other formats later, content negotiation matters more.
How would you design an endpoint for exporting posts as CSV?

If it’s a different representation of the same resource, you can use content negotiation: - GET /posts with Accept: text/csv But in practice, many clients find it easier to use a distinct endpoint: - GET /posts/export?format=csv Or: - GET /posts.csv I’d prefer: - GET /posts/export with format param or Accept header. It also allows export-specific options (columns, delimiter) without cluttering /posts.
For long-running exports, should it be async?

If exports can be large, async is better. Pattern: 1) POST /exports (or /posts/exports) Request: { "type": "posts", "filters": {...}, "format": "csv" } Response: 202 Accepted with { "id": "exp_..." } 2) GET /exports/{id} returns status: pending/running/complete/failed and download_url when complete. This avoids timeouts and allows retries.
I’m worried about over-engineering. Is it okay to start simple and evolve?

Yes, and it’s usually the right move. A good strategy: - Start with core resources: /posts, /posts/{id}, /users, /users/{id}. - Implement consistent filtering, sorting, pagination early (those are hard to change later). - Keep response formats stable and extensible (error format, envelopes if you choose them). - Add includes/fields/export/bulk later when real needs appear. The key is to avoid choices that are hard to change: pagination style, naming conventions, auth model, and canonical resource identifiers.
If I start with offset pagination now, can I migrate to cursor later?

You can, but it can be a breaking change unless you support both. Migration path: - Keep offset/limit as-is. - Add cursor pagination as an alternative: /posts?limit=20&cursor=... - Or add a param: pagination=cursor. - Deprecate offset over time. If you’re early enough, it might be better to adopt cursor now. But if simplicity is paramount, offset is okay; just be aware of future complexity.
For documentation, should the API be self-describing enough, or do I need OpenAPI?

OpenAPI (Swagger) is very useful: - Generates interactive docs and client SDKs. - Provides a contract for validation and testing. - Helps keep docs in sync. Even if the API is “simple,” OpenAPI pays off quickly. Keep it accurate: - Define schemas, parameters, error responses, auth. - Use examples heavily.
How do you recommend representing nullable fields in schema? Like optional updated_at.

In OpenAPI 3.0: - Use nullable: true (though there are nuances). In OpenAPI 3.1 (JSON Schema aligned): - Use type: [ "string", "null" ] with format: date-time. In the API itself: - Decide whether absent vs null has meaning. - Common: field is present and can be null when unknown/not set. - For PATCH semantics, be careful: null might mean “clear.”
Should I differentiate between omitted and null in PATCH?
Yes, if you want to support “clear a field.” With JSON Merge Patch: - Omitted means unchanged. - null means set to null. If some fields should not be clearable, reject null with validation error.
For enums like status, should I use strings or integers?

Strings are better for readability and forward compatibility: - "draft", "published", "archived" Integers are compact but obscure and harder to evolve without coordination. Strings also make debugging easier.
Should I namespace enum values? Like "post.published"?

Usually unnecessary. Simple strings are fine as long as the field name provides context (status on a post). Namespacing can be useful if the same field name appears across resources with different meanings, but that’s uncommon.
For multi-tenancy-like separation, is it okay to have /users/{id}/posts? I know we said /posts with filters is better, but scoping seems useful.

If scoping is a core concept, nested routes can make authorization boundaries clearer. But you can still keep /posts canonical and treat scoping as a filter. If “scope” is always required (you must always specify a user context), then /users/{id}/posts might actually be the primary collection. But if you also need global views (admin, public feed), /posts remains important. Given you expect many filters and global queries, /posts?user_id=... remains flexible. You can always add /users/{id}/posts as a convenience without making it the only way.
How would you handle “current feed” like posts from followed users? /feed?

“Feed” is more of a computed collection than a simple resource, so a dedicated endpoint is fine: - GET /feed Or: - GET /posts?feed=following But /feed is clear and avoids mixing feed ranking logic with generic listing. You can still reuse the same post representation. Support pagination and filtering where sensible: - GET /feed?limit=20&cursor=...
Should /feed be under /users/me/feed?

If feed is inherently tied to the current user, /users/me/feed is semantically precise. /feed assumes “current user” implicitly (requires auth). Either is fine. I’d choose: - /users/me/feed if you want everything user-scoped to live under /users/me. - /feed if you want a simpler top-level concept. Consistency with other “me” endpoints matters.
For “like” functionality, do you model it as /posts/{id}/likes?

Yes, likes fit well as a subresource. Patterns: - POST /posts/{id}/likes to like (creates a like by current user). - DELETE /posts/{id}/likes to unlike (removes current user’s like). - GET /posts/{id}/likes to list users who liked (if you expose that). If you need to refer to a like as a resource: - /likes/{likeId} (less common unless likes have metadata). Also consider idempotency: - POST /posts/{id}/likes should be idempotent (liking twice should not create duplicates). It can return 200 or 201 the first time, and 200 thereafter.
Would you return 409 if already liked?

You can, but idempotent behavior is usually nicer: - POST returns 201 when it created, 200 when it already existed, both with the same representation. Or always 204/200. 409 is more strict but can make clients handle more cases. For “toggle-like,” idempotency is convenient.
For permissions, do you recommend role-based fields in the user object? Or separate /permissions endpoint?

It depends on how complex permissions are. Simple: - User object includes role: "admin" | "user". - Or includes scopes array. More complex: - /users/me/permissions returns a list of capabilities (strings) the client can use to enable/disable UI. - Or include it in /users/me as "capabilities": ["post:create","post:delete"]. I’d avoid a separate endpoint unless permissions are large or frequently changing. Including capabilities in /users/me is convenient.
How do you structure consistent naming for capabilities?

Use stable, namespaced strings: - "posts:create", "posts:update", "posts:delete" - "comments:moderate" Or: - "post.create" style Pick one delimiter and resource naming style (plural vs singular). Plural resources + colon is common.
For API responses, should I include a top-level “type” field for polymorphism?

Only if you truly have polymorphic collections. If an endpoint can return different resource types in “data,” then a type discriminator helps: - { "type": "post", "id": "...", ... } But if /posts always returns posts, it’s unnecessary overhead. If you later build a /feed that mixes posts and other items, a type field is useful there.
That’s helpful. Can we circle back: if you had to pick one between /users/{id}/posts and /posts?user_id=, which would you pick for a filter-heavy API?

For a filter-heavy API, I’d pick: - GET /posts?user_id={id} Then define consistent filtering/sorting/pagination on /posts. You can still add: - GET /users/{id}/posts as an alias later if you want readability, but keep /posts as the canonical list interface.
If I keep only /posts, what about discoverability? Like how does a client know posts belong to a user?

You can make relationships explicit in representations and docs: - Post object includes user_id (and maybe an embedded author summary). - User object can include links: { "links": { "posts": "/posts?user_id=..." } } Even if you don’t implement full HATEOAS, including a “posts_url” or “links.posts” field in user responses improves discoverability without needing nested routes.
Is it okay to have “posts_url” fields, or should it always be under links?
Prefer a consistent “links” object: - "links": { "self": "...", "posts": "..." } It avoids proliferating *_url fields and keeps semantics clear.
Great. If I adopt links, should they be absolute or relative?

Relative is fine and often preferable: - "/posts/123" It keeps responses environment-agnostic (dev/staging/prod). If clients need absolute URLs, they can join with the API base URL. If you expect clients to use links directly without context (e.g., webhooks consumed outside), absolute URLs can be useful. Many APIs choose relative for internal API responses, absolute for externally shared links.
That covers most of my questions. Any final gotchas in endpoint structure that people often overlook?

A few common pitfalls: - Inconsistent pagination/sorting across endpoints (clients hate special cases). - Multiple URLs for the same resource without a clear canonical one. - Mixing nouns and verbs in paths inconsistently. - Lack of a stable error code system (makes client logic brittle). - Not reserving query param names early (sort, limit, cursor, include, fields). - Silent ignoring of unknown params (typos become bugs). If you lock down conventions now—naming, pagination, filtering—you’ll have a much easier time evolving the API.

Thanks. I’ll start drafting with /posts as the main collection, cursor pagination, sort=-created_at, and optional include. I might add /users/me as a convenience. If I run into specifics, I’ll ask again.

Sounds like a solid baseline. If you share a draft of a few endpoints plus example request/response payloads, I can help sanity-check consistency (pagination, errors, includes, and canonical URLs) before you commit.

I drafted a few endpoints and I’m not sure they’re consistent. Here’s what I have: - GET /v1/posts?limit=20&cursor=... - GET /v1/posts/{post_id} - POST /v1/posts - PATCH /v1/posts/{post_id} - DELETE /v1/posts/{post_id} - GET /v1/users/me - GET /v1/users/{user_id} - GET /v1/users/{user_id}/posts (alias) - POST /v1/posts/{post_id}/publish - POST /v1/posts/{post_id}/likes - DELETE /v1/posts/{post_id}/likes - GET /v1/posts/{post_id}/comments - POST /v1/posts/{post_id}/comments - GET /v1/comments/{comment_id} Does anything jump out as odd?

The set is mostly coherent; a few spots to tighten so you don’t create long-term inconsistencies. 1) Publish endpoint vs PATCH status - Everything else treats post state as a resource, but publish is an action endpoint. That’s not “wrong,” but it’s a fork in style. - If publish is just “set status=published and published_at=now,” I’d prefer: PATCH /v1/posts/{post_id} { "status": "published" } - If publish triggers workflows and you want it explicit, keep: POST /v1/posts/{post_id}/publish And consider symmetry: POST /v1/posts/{post_id}/unpublish (or /draft), or make it a subresource: PUT /v1/posts/{post_id}/publication (create) DELETE /v1/posts/{post_id}/publication (remove) The main “odd” part is having both PATCH for general updates and POST for a specific state change. Decide intentionally. 2) Users/{user_id}/posts alias - Fine as an alias if it’s truly identical to /posts?user_id=... including pagination params. Make sure it accepts the same query params: /v1/users/{user_id}/posts?limit=20&cursor=... - One small consideration: if you keep it, also consider /v1/users/me/posts for symmetry and ergonomics, unless you already expect clients to use /posts?mine=true. 3) Likes endpoints are good, but decide response semantics - POST /v1/posts/{id}/likes should be idempotent as discussed; document whether it returns 201 vs 200 when already liked. - If you ever want “like count” or “did I like this” without fetching the entire post, you might add: GET /v1/posts/{id}/likes (list) or GET /v1/posts/{id}/likes/me (check) Not required now, just plan that the shape won’t conflict. 4) Comments: mixed canonical vs nested is good - /v1/posts/{post_id}/comments for collection + create is consistent. - /v1/comments/{comment_id} as canonical item is consistent. - If you later add PATCH/DELETE for comments, decide whether they live at /comments/{id} (recommended) rather than nested. 5) Version prefix - /v1 everywhere is consistent. If you ever add a non-versioned health endpoint, keep it separate (e.g., /healthz) so clients don’t confuse it with API versioning. If you share example payloads (post representation, list envelope, error shape), the biggest consistency issues usually show up there: whether list endpoints always envelope, how links are represented, and whether include/fields behave the same across /posts and the alias.

Here are example responses. Single post: GET /v1/posts/01HV... (ULID) 200 { "id": "01HV...", "title": "Hello", "body": "World", "created_at": "2026-04-02T13:45:30Z", "updated_at": null, "author": { "id": "u1", "name": "Sam" }, "links": { "self": "/v1/posts/01HV..." } } List: GET /v1/posts?limit=2 200 { "data": [ { "id": "01HV1...", "title": "A", "created_at": "...", "links": { "self": "/v1/posts/01HV1..." } }, { "id": "01HV0...", "title": "B", "created_at": "...", "links": { "self": "/v1/posts/01HV0..." } } ], "meta": { "limit": 2, "next_cursor": "01HV0...", "has_more": true }, "links": { "self": "/v1/posts?limit=2", "next": "/v1/posts?limit=2&cursor=01HV0..." } } Error: 404 { "error": { "code": "not_found", "message": "Post not found", "request_id": "r123" } } I’m unsure about embedding author as object vs just author_id + include=author.

Your shapes are already close; the main inconsistency is that single ресурce responses are not enveloped but lists are. That’s acceptable, but decide if you want that asymmetry. On author embedding, you basically have three levels; pick one and be consistent: A) Always embed a small author summary (what you’re doing) - Pros: very convenient; most clients need author name. - Cons: you’ve implicitly committed to author always being present and to that embedded shape being stable. - Gotcha: if you later want sparse fieldsets, embedded objects complicate it unless you support fields[user]=... If you keep this approach, I’d rename author to author_summary or keep author but be explicit in docs that it’s a summary (id + display fields) and not the full user resource. B) Return author_id only by default; include=author to embed - Pros: consistent with “include” mechanism; lighter default payload; avoids N+1 issues on list endpoints unless include is used. - Cons: clients often do want author, so they’ll almost always request include=author, or they’ll do extra calls. If you choose this, I’d structure: - default: { ..., "author_id": "u1", "links": { "author": "/v1/users/u1" } } - with include=author: add "author": { ... } or add a side-loaded "included" array (JSON:API style). Since you’re not otherwise doing JSON:API, embedded is simpler than side-loading. C) Hybrid: include minimal author fields inline + still have author_id - Example: "author": { "id": "u1", "name": "Sam" }, and also "author_id": "u1" - Usually redundant. I’d avoid unless you have a strong reason. Given you already have include planned, I’d lean toward B for cleanliness—especially because lists are where author embedding can become expensive. But if your project is small and you know “author name is always needed,” A is pragmatic. Two more small notes on your examples: 1) updated_at: null - Fine. Just ensure you always include it (even if null) if you want stable shape. Or omit it when null; but then clients must handle missing vs null. Since you already care about PATCH null semantics, keeping it present with null is okay. 2) Cursor and ordering - Your next_cursor being the last item’s id implies sorting by id desc (ULID). Make sure you document default sort as -id or -created_at and that cursor matches it. If default sort is -created_at, but cursor uses id, you can get subtle mismatches unless you guarantee created_at aligns with ULID time. If you tell me your intended default ordering (by created_at vs by ULID id), I can suggest the most consistent cursor contract.
I was planning default sort=-created_at. But since ULID is time-ordered, I figured using id as cursor is “close enough.” Is that a bad idea?

It can be fine only if you guarantee created_at and ULID time are effectively the same ordering key. If there’s any chance they diverge, you’ll eventually see pagination anomalies (skips/duplicates) that are hard to debug. When it breaks: - Backfills/imports where created_at is in the past but the ULID is generated “now.” - Any ability to set created_at explicitly (even internal tooling). - If created_at is truncated to seconds but ULID has millisecond resolution, tie-breaking can differ. If you want default sort=-created_at, the cursor should encode the sort key: - Cursor = (created_at, id) tuple. - next query uses created_at < last_created_at OR (created_at = last_created_at AND id < last_id) with ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC. If you really want cursor to just be id, then make default sort=-id (and optionally allow sort=-created_at as an explicit request that switches cursor format). But switching cursor formats based on sort can confuse clients unless documented very clearly. Practical recommendation: - Choose one canonical ordering for pagination: - Either: default sort=-id and treat ULID as your created order. - Or: default sort=-created_at and use composite cursor (opaque token is easiest). If you keep -created_at because it’s more semantically obvious, I’d do opaque cursor: - meta.next_cursor = "eyJjcmVhdGVkX2F0Ijoi...","id":"..."} base64 Clients don’t care what’s inside; they just pass it back.
If I make the cursor opaque, should I sign/encrypt it to prevent clients from tampering?

You don’t have to, but you should decide what “tampering” would mean. Common options: 1) Opaque but not signed (base64-encoded JSON) - Pros: simplest; easy to debug. - Cons: clients can modify it. Usually that just results in 400 or weird paging, not a security issue, as long as authorization is enforced on the query. 2) Signed (HMAC) - Pros: prevents clients from forging arbitrary cursors; lets you reject modified tokens cleanly. - Cons: slightly more work; you need key rotation strategy eventually. 3) Encrypted + signed - Usually overkill unless cursor contains sensitive info (it generally shouldn’t). What I’d do: - If cursor contains only (created_at, id, sort, filters hash maybe), and you validate auth on every request anyway, signing is optional. - But signing is nice for robustness: you can detect “cursor doesn’t match this query” cases reliably. If you do sign, keep it simple: - cursor = base64url(payload) + "." + base64url(hmac(payload, secret)) - On parse: verify signature, then decode. Also consider binding cursor to the query: - If a client changes filters but reuses an old cursor, results can be nonsense. You can: - Document “cursor is only valid with the same filters/sort,” and return 400 if mismatch. - To enforce mismatch detection, include a hash of canonicalized query params in the cursor payload (and/or in the signed payload).
Good idea. How do you “canonicalize” query params for hashing? Especially arrays like tag=foo&tag=bar.

Canonicalization rules need to be deterministic and documented (even if only internally). A workable approach: 1) Decide which params are part of the “result set identity” - Include: filters (tag, user_id, status, q), sort, any scope flags. - Exclude: pagination controls (limit, cursor) and fields/include parameters if they don’t affect which items appear (they affect shape, not membership). If include can affect filtering (it shouldn’t), then include it. 2) Normalize keys and values - Lowercase parameter names if your API treats them case-insensitively (many don’t; safest is preserve exact). - Trim whitespace around values. - For booleans, normalize to "true"/"false". - For timestamps, normalize to a standard format (ISO 8601 Z). 3) Normalize repeated params / arrays - For params that are sets (tags typically): sort values lexicographically. Example: tag=bar&tag=foo becomes tag=[bar,foo] always. - For params where order matters (rare in filters): keep order. But for tags, treat as set. 4) Normalize encoding - Build a canonical query string: - Sort keys lexicographically. - For each key, append key=value pairs in sorted value order. - Use consistent percent-encoding (RFC 3986) and join with &. 5) Hash - Compute SHA-256 of the canonical string, store hex or base64url in cursor payload. Example: Input: /posts?status=published&tag=foo&tag=bar&sort=-created_at Canonical string: sort=-created_at&status=published&tag=bar&tag=foo Hash that. Then cursor payload includes: { "v": 1, "sort": "-created_at", "after": { "created_at": "...", "id": "..." }, "qhash": "..." } On request with cursor: - Recompute qhash from request params (excluding limit/cursor). - If mismatch, return 400 with error code like cursor_mismatch.
Nice. Another question: for PATCH with merge-patch, should I allow patching nested objects like author? I assume no, but then how do I prevent clients from sending it?

You generally should not allow patching derived/related objects like author via the post endpoint. How to handle it: 1) Define writable vs read-only fields - Writable: title, body, status, tags, etc. - Read-only: id, created_at, updated_at, author, author_id (if ownership immutable), links. 2) Validation behavior when read-only fields appear in PATCH Two reasonable choices: - Strict: reject with 400 validation_error, pointing to the field(s). - Lenient: ignore read-only fields silently. I strongly prefer strict for PATCH because silent ignoring can hide client bugs. So: - If payload contains "author": {...}, return 400 with details: field=author issue=read_only. 3) If you need to change ownership (rare) - Make it explicit with a dedicated endpoint or admin-only field: PATCH /posts/{id} { "author_id": "u2" } (admin) But for typical apps, keep it immutable. Implementation detail: - For merge-patch, nested objects are tricky because a client could send: { "author": { "name": "X" } } If you treat author as read-only, just reject any presence of "author" key, regardless of content.
For tags, I’m thinking tags is an array of strings. With merge-patch, if client sends { "tags": ["a","b"] } it replaces whole list. What if I want add/remove semantics?

Merge Patch is replace-oriented for arrays, so “add/remove one tag” doesn’t have a native operation. Common options: 1) Accept replacement semantics only (simplest) - PATCH replaces the entire tags array. - Clients must fetch current tags, modify, send back. - Works fine for most cases; add ETag/If-Match to avoid lost updates. 2) Provide dedicated tag mutation endpoints - POST /posts/{id}/tags { "tag": "python" } (add) - DELETE /posts/{id}/tags/{tag} (remove) This is explicit and efficient, but adds more endpoints. 3) Use JSON Patch (RFC 6902) for advanced clients - PATCH with Content-Type: application/json-patch+json - Operations like add/remove at /tags/- This is powerful but more complex. If tags are central and frequently mutated, option 2 is ergonomic. If tags are occasional and you already plan to use If-Match, option 1 is enough.
If I do DELETE /posts/{id}/tags/{tag}, how do I handle tags with spaces or slashes?

Path parameters become painful with arbitrary strings. Safer approaches: - Use a tag_id (slug) that is URL-safe (e.g., lowercase, hyphenated). - Or keep tags as free-form strings but don’t put them in the path. If tags are free-form, prefer: - DELETE /posts/{id}/tags with body { "tag": "Data Science" } (but DELETE body support issues) - Or POST /posts/{id}/tags/remove with body (pragmatic but action-y) - Or treat tag membership as a subresource with generated id: POST /posts/{id}/taggings { "tag": "Data Science" } DELETE /taggings/{tagging_id} Given the complexity, if you want tag mutation endpoints, I’d recommend making tags a controlled vocabulary with URL-safe slugs, or accept “replace entire array” and avoid per-tag endpoints.
I’ll keep replace semantics for now with ETags. On ETags, should list endpoints also return ETag?

You can, but list ETags are often low value unless the list is cacheable and you can compute a stable ETag cheaply. Item endpoints: - High value: ETag supports both caching and optimistic locking. List endpoints: - Harder because ETag must vary by query params and auth context. - Computing an accurate ETag might require scanning results or tracking a materialized version. If you still want it: - Only do it for public, non-personalized lists. - Include Vary appropriately (Authorization, etc.). - Ensure the ETag is tied to the exact response representation (including pagination window), or at least to the membership+ordering. Pragmatically: start with ETags on item endpoints only. Add list caching later if you see a need.
For optimistic locking, do you recommend If-Match on PATCH/DELETE only, or also on POST publish/like actions?

Minimum useful: - Require If-Match on PATCH /posts/{id} and DELETE /posts/{id} (or at least support it). That prevents lost updates and accidental deletes. For action endpoints: - Publish: If publish depends on current state (e.g., only draft can be published), you can enforce state with either: - If-Match (client must have latest), or - Preconditions in body: { "expected_status": "draft" } (less standard), or - Server-side check returning 409 if already published/archived. If publish is idempotent and safe, If-Match is less necessary. - Likes: generally don’t need If-Match; they’re per-user and idempotent. Concurrency issues are minimal. So: focus If-Match on modifications to primary resources (posts, maybe comments).
For 409 vs 412, when do you use which?

Use 412 Precondition Failed when the client supplied a precondition header (If-Match / If-Unmodified-Since) and it failed. Use 409 Conflict when the request is syntactically valid but conflicts with the current state/business rules, without a failed precondition header. Examples: - Client PATCH with If-Match and ETag mismatch -> 412. - Client tries to publish an archived post -> 409 (state conflict). - Client tries to create a post with duplicate unique title (if that’s a rule) -> 409. This distinction helps clients: 412 tells them “refresh and retry with latest ETag.”
Makes sense. Another design thing: should I standardize on returning 200 with representation for PATCH, or 204 no content?

Either is fine; pick one and be consistent. - 200 with updated representation: - Pros: client gets canonical server state (including computed fields, updated_at, links) without an extra GET. - Cons: more bandwidth. - 204 No Content: - Pros: lighter. - Cons: client often needs to refetch anyway; also you can’t easily return updated ETag unless you still include headers (you can include ETag with 204, but clients may still want the body). I’d choose: - PATCH returns 200 with the updated resource and ETag header. It’s simplest for clients and avoids “PATCH then GET” patterns.
For POST /posts, should I also return ETag?
Yes, if you’re using ETags for concurrency: - Return ETag for the newly created resource along with 201 and Location. That lets the client immediately do conditional updates without another GET.
Last thing: I’m thinking about consistent “links” fields. Should list items include links.self only, or also links.author, links.comments?

Keep list items minimal; include only what you know clients will use often. A practical baseline: - Always: links.self - Optional: - links.author if you’re not embedding author and you want discoverability. - links.comments if comments are a common follow-up navigation. If you include too many links, payload grows and clients may still not use them. Since you already have predictable URL patterns, links are mainly for convenience rather than necessity. If you do include them, keep naming consistent: - links: { self, author, comments, likes } And ensure they’re valid even if the user lacks permission (if permissioned, you can omit links the caller can’t use, but that makes representations vary by auth context—sometimes desirable, sometimes confusing).

When you implement include=author, pay attention to two edge cases that often bite: - Pagination + include: ensure you batch-load authors for the page (avoid N+1). - Sparse fields: if you add fields[post] later, define whether include is allowed to add fields not requested (usually yes for included resources, but not for the primary resource).
